documents.excerpt: Electrical Clause A usually restricts cover for electrical damage originating from a particular machine / component.

headings.text: 1. Electrical Clause A
Simple Meaning
Key Point
Application Sample
Facts

documents.searchText: 1. electrical clause a electrical clause a.md policy & clauses 1. electrical clause a simple meaning key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses 1. electrical clause a simple meaning electrical clause a usually restricts cover for electrical damage originating from a particular machine / component. if fire starts from a particular machine, the starter / electrical component that ignited the fire may not be payable. resultant fire damage to other property / other section may still be payable. key point separate the defective / originating electrical component from resultant fire damage. application sample facts fire started from a machine starter / electrical component. the component itself was burnt. fire spread and damaged surrounding items. suggested wording the fire appears to have originated from the electrical component / starter of the affected machine. in view of electrical clause a, the cost of repairing / replacing the originating component is not recommended. however, resultant fire damage to other affected property may be considered, subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions and excess.

documents.title: Adequacy of Sum Insured
documents.excerpt: The adequacy of the Sum Insured is a principle used to determine whether a property is insured for its true total value at the time a loss or damage occurs

headings.text: Adequacy of Sum Insured
1. Meaning of Sum Insured
2. Importance of Adequate Sum Insured
3. Factors Determining Adequate Sum Insured
4. Consequences of Under insurance
5. Consequences of Over insurance
6. How to Ensure Adequacy
7. Special Considerations

documents.searchText: adequacy of sum insured adequacy of sum insured.md policy & clauses adequacy of sum insured 1. meaning of sum insured 2. importance of adequate sum insured 3. factors determining adequate sum insured 4. consequences of under insurance 5. consequences of over insurance 6. how to ensure adequacy 7. special considerations the adequacy of the sum insured is a principle used to determine whether a property is insured for its true total value at the time a loss or damage occurs . to check for adequacy, adjusters compare the policy's sum insured against the property's actual total value, which is known as the "value at risk" . when the sum insured is inadequate (underinsurance) if the total value of the insured property is greater than the sum insured, the coverage is deemed inadequate . in this scenario, the "condition of average" applies, meaning the insured is considered to be their own insurer for the shortfall and must bear a rateable, or proportional, share of the loss . for example, if the sum insured is rm300,000.00 but the actual value at risk is rm350,000.00, the claim payout will be proportionately reduced to reflect this underinsurance . when the sum insured is adequate if the sum insured is equal to or higher than the value at risk, it is considered adequate . in this case, the condition of average does not apply, and the loss can be paid without proportional reduction, subject only to any applicable policy excesses or deductibles . how value at risk is calculated the method for calculating the required value at risk depends on the basis of the policy: reinstatement value basis: the sum insured must equal the full cost of replacing or reinstating the destroyed property with new items of the same kind and capacity, including freight and erection costs, without deducting for depreciation . some policies include a special provision where the condition of average only triggers if the sum insured is less than 85% of the actual replacement or reinstatement cost . indemnity (market value) basis: the value at risk is calculated by taking the current replacement cost and deducting an allowance for betterment, wear and tear, and depreciation . special considerations first loss policies: for policies issued on a "first loss" basis, the standard condition of average is generally waived, meaning the adequacy calculation is not applied to proportionately reduce the claim up to the specified first loss limit. knowledge notebooklm. certainly! here are the key points related to the "adequacy of sum insured" with clear headings and explanations: adequacy of sum insured 1. meaning of sum insured the sum insured is the maximum amount an insurance policy will pay in case of a claim. it represents the insured value or coverage limit for a specific risk or property. 2. importance of adequate sum insured ensures sufficient coverage to meet financial needs during a claim. prevents under insurance, which can lead to out of pocket expenses. avoids over insurance, which results in unnecessary premium costs. 3. factors determining adequate sum insured replacement cost : cost to replace or repair the property at current prices. market value : the current market value of the property or asset. liability exposure : potential legal liabilities that might require financial coverage. inflation : anticipated increase in costs over time. 4. consequences of under insurance limited payout that may not cover actual damages. financial burden on the insured to cover shortfall. 5. consequences of over insurance higher premiums without corresponding benefit. 6. how to ensure adequacy regularly review and update the sum insured based on inflation and market changes. conduct proper valuation of assets periodically. 7. special considerations in cases like health insurance, ensure coverage limits match potential medical expenses. for property insurance, cover rebuilding costs rather than market value for adequacy.

documents.title: ADJUSTER CHECKLIST
documents.excerpt: Think of a Loss Adjuster like the Insurance police. When a sudden "disaster" ruins your property's normal health, the insurance company sends an independent loss adjuster to verify exactly what happened, check if your "health plan" (insurance policy) actually covers it, and deter
documents.searchText: adjuster checklist adjuster checklist.md investigation & reference think of a loss adjuster like the insurance police. when a sudden "disaster" ruins your property's normal health, the insurance company sends an independent loss adjuster to verify exactly what happened, check if your "health plan" (insurance policy) actually covers it, and determine the exact "treatment cost" (claim payout) needed to fix the problem. here is a general overview of what a loss adjuster does, broken down simply: the adjuster’s scope when investigating/reviewing a claim is: confirm the appointment scope check the insurer/principal’s instructions. confirm whether the role is only to investigate and report, or also to assess quantum, apply policy wording, negotiate settlement, and pursue recovery. internal notes say the terms of engagement define the adjuster’s functions and scope. plan the investigation identify what must be checked. decide what documents, evidence, inspections, interviews, or specialists are needed. keep the work within the assignment scope. investigate the cause of loss determine how the loss happened. check whether the claimed cause is consistent with the evidence. consider whether there are third party recovery rights. inspect and verify the damage conduct visual inspection where possible. identify exactly what was damaged or lost. compare the insured’s version with physical evidence, records, photos, invoices, repair reports, and other supporting documents. validate the claim check whether the loss actually occurred. confirm the damaged items, quantity, specification, ownership, and connection to the incident. where physical inspection is not possible, rely on records and other evidence to support the claim. review policy coverage apply the policy wording, schedule, limits, exclusions, conditions, warranties, excess, and basis of settlement. confirm whether the claimed loss falls within the policy. assess the amount of loss calculate the true financial impact of the damage/loss. apply the correct settlement basis, such as indemnity, reinstatement, depreciation, betterment, or other policy specific method. internal notes say this must be done strictly according to the insurance contract. engage specialists where needed recommend or coordinate experts for technical issues beyond the adjuster’s general expertise, subject to the principal’s approval. give specialists a clear brief and defined scope. check compliance and conduct ensure the investigation complies with professional and legal requirements in the relevant jurisdiction. maintain impartiality, proper documentation, and fair review of the insured’s claim. report findings report on cause, damage, policy response, quantum, issues, outstanding documents, reserve recommendation, recovery potential, and settlement recommendation. negotiate/settle only if instructed settlement negotiation is within scope only where the principal’s instructions allow it. the internal notes list negotiation and settlement as part of the broader adjusting process, but also state that the actual steps depend on the assignment scope. in simple terms: the adjuster investigates what happened, verifies what was damaged, checks whether the policy responds, assesses how much is payable, reports to the insurer, and only negotiates/settles or pursues recovery if that is within the appointment scope. 1. the adjuster investigation and review scope when an adjuster gets assigned to a case, they follow a strict, step by step diagnostic process to gather all the facts before recommending a payout. their scope includes: interview the insured / participant and eyewitnesses: they always start by taking a detailed clinical history, sitting down with the affected people to gather information regarding the exact circumstances of the incident. establish the alleged cause of loss: they act like sleep detectives to confirm the exact root trigger of the breakdown. for example, they will check independent weather websites to verify a storm actually happened, or review cctv footage and physical damage to confirm a break in occurred. physical site inspection: they physically visit the site to examine the "patient." they take measurements, photograph the damage, and document all the affected structures or machines. procure relevant documents: they collect and review the "medical records," which include police reports, fire brigade investigation findings, machine maintenance logs, and financial records. consult external specialists: if a diagnosis is highly complex, like a mysterious machine explosion or a massive fire, the adjuster will appoint specialized "neurologists," such as forensic engineers or independent building contractors, to test the equipment or provide an accurate repair cost. 2. causes and types of claims adjusters are trained to diagnose a wide variety of sudden shocks to a building's system. the common "disruptions" they investigate include: fires and explosions: often caused by electrical short circuits or gas leaks. lightning and power surges: massive electrical waves that fry the delicate "brains" (circuit boards) of modern equipment. windstorms and floods: heavy downpours that blow off roofs or inundate buildings, soaking inventory and furniture. thefts and burglaries: culprits breaking into premises to steal money, stock, or equipment. machinery breakdowns: unforeseen physical damage to industrial machines. 3. the effects (the prognosis) the adjuster’s final report determines the ultimate outcome of the claim. approval: if the loss is valid, the adjuster calculates a fair repair or replacement cost and recommends that the insurer pays it. rejection: if the adjuster discovers the damage was simply caused by old age, everyday wear and tear, or poor maintenance, they will advise the insurer to reject the claim, treating it like a preventable lifestyle issue rather than a sudden accident. 4. key terms and concepts to calculate the exact payout, adjusters use a specific set of clinical rules: policy liability: the official decision confirming that your specific insurance plan actually covers the exact type of accident that happened. adequacy of sum insured (underinsurance): if you told the insurance company your building was worth much less than it actually is to save on premiums, the adjuster will apply the "condition of average" and proportionately shrink your payout. depreciation (natural aging): if the building or item is older and hasn't been completely rebuilt yet, the adjuster deducts a percentage from the payout to account for everyday wear and tear. betterment (unfair upgrades): if a repair replaces an old, basic machine with a vastly superior, upgraded model, the adjuster deducts a cost so you don't unfairly profit from the accident. excess / deductible (the co pay): a standard, set amount (like rm200 or rm5,000) that the policy requires you to bear yourself. the adjuster automatically deducts this from your final claim payout.

documents.title: Agreed Value — Meaning
documents.excerpt: Agreed value means the value of the insured property is agreed upfront between the Insured and Insurer.

headings.text: Agreed Value — Meaning
Simple Meaning
Key Point
Total Loss
Partial Loss
Agreed Value Calculator
Adjuster Review

documents.searchText: agreed value — meaning agreed value clause.md policy & clauses agreed value — meaning simple meaning key point total loss partial loss agreed value calculator adjuster review suggested report wording endorsement clauses agreed value — meaning simple meaning agreed value means the value of the insured property is agreed upfront between the insured and insurer. for building cover, the agreed value is usually reflected by the sum insured stated in the policy / certificate schedule. it is different from market value because settlement is not mainly based on open market selling price at the time of loss. it is intended to reduce dispute over value after a loss, provided the insured information was declared fully and accurately. key point agreed value does not mean every claim is automatically paid in full. it means the basis of value is agreed upfront, subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions, limits, excess, and accuracy of declaration. total loss if the building is totally destroyed, settlement is generally based on the agreed value / sum insured stated in the schedule. this remains subject to: policy terms and conditions excess salvage limits accuracy of declaration any applicable policy proviso partial loss if only part of the building is damaged, settlement is usually based on the reasonable cost of repair / reinstatement. the repair should restore the damaged portion using materials of similar kind and quality. it should not allow betterment or improvement beyond the original condition unless policy allows. agreed value calculator for building cover, agreed value may be determined using an agreed value calculator. the insured must declare the required information fully and accurately. important details include: built up area construction type building details location / risk address other information required by the insurer adjuster review check whether agreed value clause applies. check whether it applies to building only or other insured property. check the sum insured stated in the schedule. check whether the insured used the agreed value basis instead of market value. check whether the declaration appears accurate. check whether there is any under declaration issue. check whether the loss is total loss or partial loss. check whether repair cost is reasonable. check excess, salvage, limits, exclusions, and conditions. suggested report wording the policy / certificate is noted to be subject to agreed value basis for the building section. under this basis, the sum insured stated in the schedule represents the agreed value of the building, subject to the terms, conditions, exclusions, limits and excess of the policy / certificate. for partial loss, the claim is assessed based on the reasonable cost of repairing / reinstating the damaged portion with materials of similar kind and quality. we have therefore assessed the loss on repair / reinstatement basis, subject to the agreed value clause and policy conditions.

documents.searchText: agreed value vs reinstatement value vs market value differ from rvc and market value clause.md policy & clauses agreed value vs reinstatement value vs market value simple difference 1. agreed value meaning claim effect adjuster note 2. reinstatement value / rvc meaning claim effect adjuster note 3. market value meaning claim effect adjuster note quick comparison table easy way to remember suggested report wording agreed value reinstatement value / rvc market value agreed value clause. agreed value vs reinstatement value vs market value simple difference agreed value value is agreed upfront between insured and insurer. usually shown as the sum insured in the schedule. main purpose is to reduce dispute over value after loss. still subject to policy terms, conditions, excess, limits, and accuracy of declaration. reinstatement value / rvc settlement is based on cost to repair, replace, or reinstate the damaged property on a new for old basis. depreciation is usually not deducted. however, the insured normally must actually reinstate / repair / replace before full rvc benefit is payable. no betterment should be allowed unless policy allows. market value settlement is based on the property’s value immediately before the loss. depreciation, age, condition, wear and tear, and market demand are considered. it puts the insured back to the same financial position before loss, not better. 2. reinstatement value / rvc meaning rvc means settlement is based on reinstating the damaged property to a condition substantially the same as, but not better than, when new. it is commonly called “new for old”. depreciation is usually not deducted if the rvc condition is complied with. claim effect insured must usually repair / replace / reinstate the damaged property. full rvc benefit is normally only payable after reinstatement is completed and supported by invoices. if the insured does not reinstate, settlement may fall back to indemnity / market value basis. betterment must still be deducted if the replacement improves the property beyond its original condition. adjuster note check whether rvc memorandum / clause is endorsed. check whether the item qualifies for rvc. check whether reinstatement was actually done. check invoices, receipts, completion, and reasonableness of costs. deduct excess, salvage, betterment, and any previous payment. 3. market value meaning market value means the value of the property immediately before the loss. it considers age, condition, depreciation, wear and tear, and market value. it is closer to indemnity basis. claim effect insured is compensated for the pre loss value, not new replacement cost. depreciation is applied. if an item is old, damaged, obsolete, or worn, the payable amount may be much lower than replacement cost. adjuster note check age and condition. check market comparison / valuation. apply depreciation. deduct excess and salvage. apply betterment if new model/features taken place. quick comparison table basis main idea depreciation? need to reinstate? best used for agreed value value agreed upfront usually not value disputed, but still subject to policy not necessarily for total loss; partial loss still adjusted building agreed value / special declared value rvc / reinstatement value new for old repair or replacement usually no depreciation if complied with yes, usually required building, machinery, equipment, property reinstatement market value value immediately before loss yes no older property, indemnity settlement, non rvc settlement easy way to remember agreed value “we agreed the value before the loss.” reinstatement value “we pay to reinstate / replace with new, subject to reinstatement.” market value “we pay what it was worth just before the loss.” suggested report wording agreed value the policy is noted to be arranged on agreed value basis for the building section. the sum insured stated in the schedule represents the agreed value, subject to policy terms, conditions, limits, excess, and accuracy of declaration. reinstatement value / rvc the policy is noted to be subject to reinstatement value memorandum. accordingly, the loss is assessed on repair / reinstatement basis, subject to the insured actually reinstating the damaged property and producing satisfactory supporting invoices. market value the policy is subject to market value / indemnity basis. accordingly, depreciation is applied to reflect the age, condition, and pre loss value of the damaged property.

documents.title: Alteration and Repair Clause
documents.excerpt: Alteration and Repair Clause allows workers to be in or around premises to carry out alterations / repairs.

headings.text: Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: alteration and repair clause alteration and repair clause.md policy & clauses key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses simple meaning alteration and repair clause allows workers to be in or around premises to carry out alterations / repairs. it prevents ordinary repair / alteration activity from automatically prejudicing cover. still subject to policy conditions and risk increase. key point check whether the works were normal repairs / alteration or major construction risk. application sample facts fire / water damage occurred while contractor was carrying out repair works. workers were present at premises. policy contains alteration and repair clause. suggested wording the policy is noted to contain alteration and repair clause. the presence of workers carrying out repair / alteration works may not by itself prejudice the policy. however, we recommend insurers / operators consider whether the works materially increased the risk or fall outside the scope of the clause.

documents.searchText: application samples average applies.md policy & clauses condition of average formula calculation application samples 1. building underinsured facts suggested wording 2. stock underinsured facts suggested wording 3. adequacy not reviewed due to appraisement clause facts suggested wording adjustment order — average, limit, excess normal order simple formula example 1 — average applies, below policy limit calculation net adjusted loss example 2 — average applies, exceeds policy limit calculation net adjusted loss report wording key point adequacy of sum insured. report layout sample condition of average sum insured: rm[amount] value at risk: rm[amount] percentage insured: [percentage]% adjusted loss before average: rm[amount] application of average: rm[amount] excess: rm[amount] net adjusted loss: rm[amount] formula adjusted loss x sum insured / value at risk calculation rm[adjusted loss] x rm[sum insured] / rm[value at risk] rm[amount before excess] less: excess rm[amount] net adjusted loss rm[amount] application samples 1. building underinsured facts sum insured: rm800,000 value at risk: rm1,000,000 adjusted repair cost: rm100,000 excess: rm1,000 suggested wording the building sum insured is rm800,000.00 against the assessed value at risk of rm1,000,000.00. the property is therefore underinsured by 20%. average has been applied to the adjusted repair cost of rm100,000.00. the adjusted loss is reduced proportionately to rm80,000.00 before deduction of excess. 2. stock underinsured facts sum insured: rm300,000 value at risk: rm600,000 adjusted stock loss: rm120,000 suggested wording the sum insured for stock is rm300,000.00 against the assessed stock value at risk of rm600,000.00. the sum insured represents 50% of the value at risk. accordingly, average has been applied and the adjusted stock loss of rm120,000.00 is reduced to rm60,000.00 before application of excess. 3. adequacy not reviewed due to appraisement clause facts adjusted loss: rm3,800 appraisement clause limit: rm5,000 suggested wording as the adjusted loss is below the appraisement clause limit of rm5,000.00, we have not reviewed adequacy of sum insured / value at risk. no average has been applied. the claim is adjusted based on the verified loss, subject to policy terms and excess. adjustment order — average, limit, excess normal order 1. check policy liability 2. assess gross adjusted loss 3. apply average / underinsurance, if applicable 4. apply policy limit / sub limit, if applicable 5. deduct excess / deductible 6. net adjusted loss simple formula adjusted loss x sum insured / value at risk = loss after average then compare with policy limit: if loss after average is below policy limit: use loss after average if loss after average exceeds policy limit: cap at policy limit then deduct excess: capped / adjusted amount excess = net payable example 1 — average applies, below policy limit adjusted loss: rm100,000 sum insured: rm500,000 value at risk: rm1,000,000 policy limit: rm80,000 excess: rm5,000 calculation average: rm100,000 x rm500,000 / rm1,000,000 = rm50,000 policy limit: rm50,000 is below rm80,000 limit use rm50,000 less excess: rm50,000 rm5,000 = rm45,000 net adjusted loss rm45,000 example 2 — average applies, exceeds policy limit adjusted loss: rm200,000 sum insured: rm800,000 value at risk: rm1,000,000 policy limit: rm100,000 excess: rm5,000 calculation average: rm200,000 x rm800,000 / rm1,000,000 = rm160,000 policy limit: rm160,000 exceeds rm100,000 limit cap at rm100,000 less excess: rm100,000 rm5,000 = rm95,000 net adjusted loss rm95,000 report wording the adjusted loss before application of average is rm[amount]. as the sum insured of rm[amount] is inadequate against the assessed value at risk of rm[amount], average is applied as follows: rm[adjusted loss] x rm[sum insured] / rm[value at risk] = rm[loss after average] the amount after average is then subject to the applicable policy limit / sub limit of rm[amount]. after deduction of policy excess / deductible of rm[amount], the net adjusted loss is rm[amount]. key point rule of thumb : average limit excess/deductibles apply average first. then apply policy limit / sub limit. then deduct excess / deductible. certainly! here's a clear summary of the "average applies" rule based on the starred blocks: rule of thumb for applying average: 1. apply average first: calculate the proportionate value by applying the average (or co insurance) clause to the declared or assessed value of the item or property. 2. apply policy limit / sub limit next: after averaging, check whether this adjusted value exceeds any policy limits or sub limits. if it does, limit the claim amount to these specified limits. 3. deduct excess / deductible last: finally, subtract any deductible or excess amount applicable to arrive at the payable claim amount. in summary: average → limit/sub limit → deductible/excess this sequence ensures that you first adjust for any under or over insurance (average), then respect policy constraints (limits/sub limits), and finally account for deductibles or excesses before arriving at the final payable amount.

documents.searchText: appraisement clause — claim notes appraisement clause.md policy & clauses appraisement clause — claim notes simple meaning easy way to remember key point application samples 1. small building repair below limit facts application suggested wording 2. small contents loss below limit facts application suggested wording 3. loss above appraisement limit facts application suggested wording 4. initial estimate below limit but later increased facts application suggested wording 5. claim below limit but liability doubtful facts application suggested wording 6. claim below limit but excluded item facts application suggested wording 7. multiple items under same loss facts application suggested wording 8. stock loss below limit facts application suggested wording report wording bank clause applies clause does not apply pending final quantum liability issue still exists short wording protective wording report wording bank clause applies clause does not apply pending final quantum liability issue still exists short wording protective wording endorsement clauses appraisement clause — claim notes simple meaning appraisement clause is a clause that allows small losses to be adjusted without checking adequacy of sum insured / value at risk. in simple terms: if the loss is below the clause limit, we do not need to apply average / underinsurance calculation. we still adjust the damage amount. we still check policy liability, cause of loss, documents, excess, exclusions and limits. it is mainly to simplify small claims. easy way to remember normal claim: check sum insured vs value at risk. if underinsured, apply average. appraisement clause applies: loss is small / below stated limit. no need to check adequacy of sum insured. no average applied. adjust the loss normally. key point appraisement clause does not mean the claim is automatically payable. it only affects the need to check adequacy / average. coverage still depends on: insured peril policy terms exclusions excess limit supporting documents reasonable repair / replacement cost application samples 1. small building repair below limit facts storm damaged small section of roof. repair cost rm3,800. policy endorsed with appraisement clause up to rm5,000. sum insured adequacy not checked. application loss is below appraisement limit. average / underinsurance calculation not required. claim still adjusted based on reasonable repair cost. suggested wording the policy is noted to be endorsed with appraisement clause. as the adjusted loss is below rm5,000.00, we have not looked into the adequacy of the sum insured / value at risk. the loss is therefore adjusted based on the reasonable repair cost, subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions and excess. 3. loss above appraisement limit facts fire damaged stock and fittings. adjusted loss rm18,000. appraisement clause limit rm5,000. application clause does not help. need to check value at risk and sum insured. apply average if underinsured. suggested wording although the policy is endorsed with appraisement clause, the adjusted loss exceeds the clause limit. accordingly, we have reviewed the adequacy of the sum insured / value at risk. any underinsurance / average condition is applied where applicable. 4. initial estimate below limit but later increased facts initial repair estimate rm4,500. later invoice / revised quotation rm7,200. clause limit rm5,000. application once loss exceeds limit, appraisement may no longer apply. need to check adequacy. suggested wording the initial estimate was below the appraisement clause limit. however, the revised claim amount exceeds the said limit. we have therefore reviewed the adequacy of the sum insured / value at risk and applied average where applicable. 5. claim below limit but liability doubtful facts alleged burglary loss rm3,000. appraisement clause limit rm5,000. no evidence of forcible entry. application appraisement only deals with adequacy. it does not override liability issue. suggested wording although the adjusted amount is below the appraisement clause limit, the clause only relates to the requirement to check adequacy of sum insured. policy liability remains subject to confirmation of the insured peril. in the absence of evidence of forcible and violent entry, liability remains doubtful. 6. claim below limit but excluded item facts damage amount rm1,800. item excluded under policy. appraisement clause endorsed. application clause does not make excluded item payable. suggested wording the appraisement clause does not override the exclusions under the policy. although the amount claimed is below the clause limit, the item claimed appears excluded / not insured. we recommend insurers / operators maintain reservation of rights on policy liability. 7. multiple items under same loss facts several damaged items. total adjusted loss rm4,900. clause limit rm5,000. application check whether limit applies per claim / per item / per occurrence. if per claim and total below limit, no adequacy check. if total exceeds limit, check adequacy. suggested wording the total adjusted loss under this occurrence is rm4,900.00. as the amount is below the appraisement clause limit, we have not reviewed adequacy of sum insured. this is subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation that the limit applies per occurrence. 8. stock loss below limit facts flood damaged small quantity of stock. adjusted stock loss rm4,200. appraisement clause limit rm5,000. application no stock var check if below limit and clause applies. still verify stock quantity and cost. suggested wording the adjusted stock loss is below the appraisement clause limit. we have therefore not looked into the adequacy of the sum insured for stock. the adjustment is based on stock records, purchase cost and damaged quantities verified. report wording bank clause applies the policy is endorsed with appraisement clause. as the adjusted loss is below rm[limit], we have not looked into the adequacy of the sum insured / value at risk. no average has been applied. clause does not apply the adjusted loss exceeds the appraisement clause limit. we have therefore reviewed the adequacy of sum insured / value at risk. average is applied where applicable. pending final quantum the applicability of the appraisement clause is subject to final adjusted quantum. if the final adjusted loss exceeds rm[limit], adequacy of sum insured will have to be reviewed. liability issue still exists the appraisement clause only relates to adequacy of sum insured. it does not affect policy liability, exclusions, excess or proof of loss. short wording in view that the adjusted loss is below the appraisement clause limit, adequacy of sum insured has not been reviewed. protective wording our non review of adequacy of sum insured is strictly on the basis that the adjusted loss falls below the appraisement clause limit and is subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation of the applicable clause wording. report wording bank clause applies the policy is endorsed with appraisement clause. as the adjusted loss is below rm[limit], we have not looked into the adequacy of the sum insured / value at risk. no average has been applied. clause does not apply the adjusted loss exceeds the appraisement clause limit. we insured. it does not affect policy liability, exclusions, excess or proof of loss. short wording in view that the adjusted loss is below the appraisement clause limit, adequacy of sum insured has not been reviewed. protective wording our non review of adequacy of sum insured is strictly on the basis that the adjusted loss falls below the appraisement clause limit and is subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation of the applicable clause wording.

documents.searchText: cause / mode of entry burglary.md peril playbooks circumstances cause / mode of entry questions to ask important point policy / certificate liability what to check investigation circumstances what happened? theft / burglary / attempted burglary / robbery / missing stock / missing equipment. when did it happen? date and time premises were last secured. date and time loss was discovered. date and time police report was lodged. where did it happen? insured premises / risk address. specific area affected: shop front store room warehouse office cashier counter safe room yard container server room how did entry take place? forced door broken padlock cut grille damaged roller shutter broken glass roof entry wall opening window entry use of duplicate key no visible force what was stolen? stock machinery tools cash computers cable fixtures vehicle parts documents personal effects who discovered the loss? owner staff guard tenant building management police neighbour policy / certificate liability what to check is the policy burglary / theft / all risks? is the risk location correct? is the stolen property insured under the schedule? is there a limit for stock / cash / property in open / property outside building? is forcible and violent entry required? is there any alarm warranty? is there any watchman warranty? is there any safe / cash warranty? is there any unattended premises condition? was the premises vacant or unoccupied? was the loss notified on time? was the police report lodged promptly? are there exclusions for employee theft / mysterious disappearance / unexplained loss? is there any excess / deductible?

documents.searchText: cecr claim — investigation aspect cecr completed engineering.md peril playbooks cecr claim — investigation aspect simple meaning key point circumstances what happened? suggested wording completed risk verification what to check? documents to request suggested wording cause investigation what to establish? technical documents to request suggested wording policy / certificate liability what to check? suggested wording site inspection what to inspect? photos to take suggested wording maintenance / gradual deterioration review what to check? documents to request suggested wording quantum / adjustment what to check? documents to request suggested wording application samples 1. gabion damaged by heavy rain / river current facts investigation suggested wording 2. embankment depression and cracks with no insured peril facts investigation suggested wording 3. retaining wall collapse after prolonged rain facts investigation suggested wording 4. slope failure / landslip facts investigation suggested wording 5. drainage / culvert washout facts investigation suggested wording 6. completed bridge / access road damaged by impact facts investigation suggested wording 7. damage discovered long after completion facts investigation suggested wording report wording bank opening completed works verification cause pending insured peril not apparent heavy rain / water current maintenance issue quantum liability reserved documents checklist basic cause quantum key reminder investigation cecr claim — investigation aspect simple meaning cecr means civil engineering completed risks. it normally relates to completed civil engineering works after construction is completed. examples: bridge road access road embankment slope retaining wall gabion wall drainage structure culvert railway / track related civil works riverbank protection works completed infrastructure key point cecr is different from car. car focuses on contract works during construction. cecr focuses on completed civil engineering property after completion. investigation must prove: what completed structure was damaged when it was completed whether it is listed / insured under the cecr policy what caused the damage whether the cause is an insured peril whether damage is sudden and accidental whether it is not merely wear and tear / gradual deterioration / maintenance issue completed risk verification what to check? is the damaged structure already completed? when was it completed? was it handed over? is it within the policy period? is it listed in the cecr policy schedule? is the risk location correct? is the damaged section part of the insured civil engineering property? was the structure maintained after completion? was there any previous damage / repair? was there any ongoing construction at the same location? documents to request cecr policy / certificate schedule list of insured completed works completion certificate handover certificate letter of award for original works contract agreement for original works bill of quantities as built drawings design drawings construction drawings location plan site plan maintenance records inspection records previous repair records photos before and after loss suggested wording we have requested completion documents, as built drawings, location plan and maintenance records to verify whether the damaged structure forms part of the insured completed civil engineering works. cause investigation what to establish? what is the immediate cause? what is the proximate cause? was there an insured peril? was damage sudden and accidental? was it due to flood / heavy rain / river current? was it due to erosion or scouring? was it due to landslip / slope failure? was it due to settlement? was it due to design defect? was it due to construction defect? was it due to poor compaction? was it due to lack of maintenance? was it due to wear and tear / gradual deterioration? was it due to pre existing condition? was it due to failure of drainage? was it due to external impact? was it due to authority / third party works? technical documents to request engineer’s report consultant’s report geotechnical report soil investigation report hydrology report drainage assessment river level / rainfall data weather records authority report maintenance inspection report incident report photos showing cause and damage drone photos, if available survey report / level survey condition assessment report previous inspection records suggested wording the proximate cause of damage remains subject to technical review. we have requested engineer’s report, maintenance records, rainfall / river level data and previous inspection records to determine whether the damage was caused by an insured peril or by gradual deterioration / maintenance issue. policy / certificate liability what to check? policy / certificate number policy period insured / participant name risk location description of insured completed works sum insured limit / sub limit deductible / excess premium / contribution warranty exclusions endorsements whether damaged property is listed whether cause is covered whether damage was sudden and accidental whether maintenance / deterioration exclusion applies whether design / workmanship defect exclusion applies whether erosion / scouring / flood is covered or excluded whether landslip / subsidence is covered or excluded whether the loss falls within policy period suggested wording policy liability is subject to confirmation that the damaged structure is insured under the cecr policy and that the proximate cause of damage falls within the scope of cover. our comments are subject to the policy terms, exclusions, conditions, limits and deductible. site inspection what to inspect? exact damaged location extent of damage type of completed structure condition of surrounding area signs of erosion / scouring signs of water flow / water mark condition of drainage condition of slope / embankment cracks / depression / settlement whether damage is fresh or old previous repair marks maintenance condition accessibility and safety whether temporary protection was carried out any nearby construction / third party works photos to take general view close up damage upstream / downstream view slope / embankment condition drainage outlet riverbank / watercourse cracks and settlement water mark failed section adjacent undamaged section repair / temporary mitigation works measurement photos suggested wording during our inspection, we observed damage to [structure] at [location]. the affected area showed signs of [erosion / scouring / settlement / cracking / collapse]. further technical review is required to determine whether the damage resulted from an insured peril or from gradual deterioration / maintenance issue. maintenance / gradual deterioration review what to check? was the completed structure maintained? any routine inspection? any previous defects? any earlier cracks / settlement? any complaints before loss? any blocked drainage? any vegetation / root issue? any erosion seen before event? any temporary repair done before? any lack of maintenance? any design / construction deficiency? documents to request maintenance schedule inspection checklist repair records complaint records previous photos authority inspection records maintenance contractor report drainage cleaning records slope monitoring records riverbank monitoring records suggested wording we have requested maintenance and inspection records to determine whether the damage occurred suddenly or developed progressively over time. if the damage is found to be due to gradual deterioration, lack of maintenance or pre existing defects, policy liability may be affected. quantum / adjustment what to check? scope of repair whether repair is like for like whether betterment is included whether temporary works are included whether emergency mitigation cost is reasonable whether design improvement is included whether original construction rate is available whether repair cost is supported by quotation / invoice whether engineer certified the repair scope whether policy limit / deductible applies whether any salvage / recovery exists documents to request repair quotation contractor’s breakdown engineer’s repair recommendation method statement for repair bill of quantities original contract rates tax invoice / receipt, if repaired photos after repair completion certificate for repair temporary protection cost measurement sheet consultant certification suggested wording the repair cost is reviewed based on reasonable reinstatement of the damaged section only. any improvement, betterment, strengthening beyond original design or unrelated maintenance work should be separated from the insured repair cost. application samples 1. gabion damaged by heavy rain / river current facts gabion wall constructed at riverbank. heavy rain caused rapid rise of river water. strong current eroded / weakened support. gabion damaged. investigation confirm gabion is insured completed work. check completion date. check policy period. check rainfall / river level. inspect scouring / erosion. request engineer report. check whether maintenance issue exists. review repair scope. suggested wording the claim concerns damage to the gabion constructed at the riverbank of the insured premises. based on available information, the damage appears related to heavy rain and rapid rise of river water which caused erosion / weakening of support to the gabion. subject to policy terms, exclusions, premium warranty and deductible, the claim may be considered if the cause is accepted as an insured peril. 2. embankment depression and cracks with no insured peril facts embankment along access road shows depression and cracks. structure was completed about 2 years earlier. no clear flood, landslip, erosion or external insured event. no evidence of soil erosion at time of discovery. investigation check whether damage is gradual settlement. check construction / compaction records. check maintenance records. check rainfall / flood records. check engineer’s opinion. check whether insured peril is evidenced. suggested wording the damage consists of depression and cracks at the embankment along the access road. based on documents presently available, there is no clear evidence that the damage was caused by an insured peril. in the absence of evidence of flood, landslip, erosion or sudden accidental event, policy liability is not apparent, subject to insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. 3. retaining wall collapse after prolonged rain facts retaining wall collapsed after prolonged rain. need to determine whether caused by insured event or poor drainage / design defect. investigation inspect drainage condition. check weep holes. check design drawings. check soil / backfill condition. check rainfall record. request engineer report. check previous cracks / bulging. check maintenance records. suggested wording the retaining wall reportedly collapsed following prolonged rainfall. we have requested engineer’s report, design drawings, drainage records and rainfall data to establish whether the collapse was caused by an insured peril or by design / construction / maintenance issue. policy liability is reserved pending technical confirmation. 4. slope failure / landslip facts slope slipped near completed civil work. damage affected access road / embankment. investigation check slope design. check rainfall data. check drainage system. check soil movement. check previous slope monitoring. check whether landslip is covered. check whether damage is sudden or progressive. suggested wording the damage appears to involve slope movement / landslip affecting the completed civil engineering works. we have requested geotechnical report, rainfall data, slope monitoring records and maintenance records. liability is subject to confirmation that landslip / slope failure is covered and that the damage was not due to gradual deterioration or lack of maintenance. 5. drainage / culvert washout facts culvert / drainage structure damaged after heavy water flow. possible blockage or undersized drainage. investigation inspect culvert inlet / outlet. check debris blockage. check water flow direction. check design capacity. check maintenance / cleaning records. check rainfall data. check repair scope. suggested wording the claim concerns damage to the drainage / culvert structure following heavy water flow. we have requested maintenance records, rainfall data and engineer’s comments to determine whether the damage was caused by an insured event or by blockage / maintenance / design issue. policy liability is reserved pending further review. 6. completed bridge / access road damaged by impact facts completed bridge / road structure damaged by vehicle / machinery impact. third party may be responsible. investigation check police report. identify responsible vehicle / party. inspect impact marks. confirm structure is insured. request repair quotation. consider recovery / subrogation. suggested wording the completed civil engineering structure was reportedly damaged by impact from [vehicle / machinery]. we have requested police report, third party details, photographs and repair quotation. subject to policy terms, the claim may be considered, with recovery action to be pursued against the responsible third party where applicable. 7. damage discovered long after completion facts damage discovered years after completion. cause unclear. possible gradual deterioration. investigation check completion date. check policy period. check inspection history. check previous defects. check maintenance records. check whether damage happened during policy period. get engineer opinion on age of damage. suggested wording the damaged structure was completed several years prior to discovery of the loss. we have requested maintenance records, inspection records and engineer’s opinion to determine whether the damage occurred suddenly during the policy period or developed gradually over time. policy liability is reserved pending confirmation of cause and timing of damage. report wording bank opening we were instructed to investigate a civil engineering completed risks claim involving damage to completed civil engineering works at the risk location. completed works verification we have requested completion documents, as built drawings and policy schedule to confirm whether the damaged structure forms part of the insured completed works. cause pending the proximate cause of damage remains subject to technical review and supporting evidence. insured peril not apparent based on documents presently available, there is no clear indication that the damage was caused by an insured peril. policy liability is therefore not apparent at this stage. heavy rain / water current the damage appears to have been caused by heavy rain / rapid rise of water level / strong current which eroded and weakened support to the affected structure. maintenance issue the available evidence suggests possible gradual deterioration / lack of maintenance / pre existing condition. policy liability may be affected, subject to policy wording and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. quantum our adjustment is based on reasonable cost to reinstate the damaged section only. any betterment, improvement, strengthening or unrelated maintenance work should be excluded or separately considered. liability reserved policy liability is reserved pending confirmation of cause, policy coverage, insured property description, exclusions, deductible and insurers’ / operators’ final position. documents checklist basic completed claim form cecr policy / certificate schedule participant / insured statement incident report photos of damage location plan site plan as built drawings completion certificate handover certificate letter of award contract agreement bill of quantities original construction drawings maintenance records inspection records repair quotation engineer / consultant report cause weather report rainfall data river level data hydrology report geotechnical report soil investigation report drainage assessment slope assessment survey report authority report previous inspection records previous repair records quantum repair quotation contractor cost breakdown boq rates measurement sheet repair method statement consultant certification tax invoice / receipt photos after repair salvage / recovery details, if any key reminder for cecr, always ask: is this a completed civil engineering work? is it listed / insured? when was it completed? did damage occur during policy period? what is the proximate cause? is there an insured peril? is it sudden and accidental? or is it gradual deterioration / maintenance / design issue?

sales records stock movement records disposal certificate salvage value important record flood level and affected area clearly. separate flood damage from pre existing damage / poor storage. water damage basic documents completed claim form photos of water damage photos of source of leak incident report repairer / plumber report cause of leakage report repair quotation tax invoice / receipt, if repaired purchase invoices for damaged items maintenance records service records tenancy agreement, if landlord / tenant issue management office report, if strata / building management involved cctv footage, if relevant if burst pipe / leakage plumber report photos of burst pipe / leaking fitting pipe repair invoice quotation for resultant damage confirmation whether pipe repair itself is claimed trace and access cost breakdown, if applicable if third party / neighbour unit involved claim letter from third party photos of third party damage repair quotation proof of ownership management office report investigation report on source of water important separate: source repair trace and access resultant water damage wear and tear / maintenance issue fidelity guarantee (fg) basic documents report employee details: full name ic number position department employment date resignation / termination date, if any employment contract / appointment letter job description payroll records hr file / disciplinary record access authority / approval limit system access log cctv footage, if relevant audit report forensic accounting report, if any bank statements cash book ledger payment vouchers receipts invoices delivery orders stock records, if stock misappropriation customer / supplier account statements reconciliation records evidence of shortage / misappropriation recovery records, if any letter of demand / admission letter, if any legal correspondence, if any if cash / fund misappropriation bank statements cash count record daily collection record receipt books payment vouchers cheque records online banking transaction records approval trail accounting system report if stock misappropriation stock listing stock count report inventory movement records delivery orders sales records purchase records cctv footage warehouse access records important need to prove dishonest / fraudulent act by employee. need to confirm employee falls within policy definition. need to establish date of discovery and period of loss. need to check whether loss falls within discovery period / policy period. need to deduct any recovery from employee / guarantor / salary / security deposit. electronic equipment (ee) basic documents completed claim form policy / certificate schedule insured / participant’s written statement photos of damaged equipment incident report equipment details: make model serial number capacity / specification year of manufacture purchase invoice asset register warranty documents maintenance records service records repair history technician / specialist report cause of damage report diagnostic report repair quotation replacement quotation parts quotation labour quotation tax invoice / receipt, if repaired confirmation whether equipment is repairable salvage value, if beyond repair disposal record, if disposed ups / avr / surge protection records, if power surge suspected lightning / tnb report, if lightning suspected cctv / access record, if accidental damage / theft suspected if computer / server / it equipment hardware diagnostic report confirmation whether physical damage occurred data recovery quotation, if claimed software licence documents, if claimed backup records it incident report server / system log maintenance agreement if medical / specialist equipment manufacturer / authorised service centre report calibration record service contract usage log breakdown / error code report replacement recommendation important need to separate physical equipment damage from data / software issue. data / software loss alone may not be covered unless policy extension applies. need technical report confirming cause of damage. for lightning / power surge, request evidence of surge / lightning and damaged component report. for wear and tear / gradual deterioration, liability may be doubtful. key reminder always ask only documents relevant to the claim facts. do not request everything blindly. first confirm: what happened? what property affected? which policy section? what cause? what quantum basis? any third party / recovery issue?
