documents.searchText: contractors' all risks (car) claim — investigation aspect car contractor all risk.md peril playbooks contractors' all risks (car) claim — investigation aspect simple meaning key point circumstances what happened? when did it happen? where did it happen? who was involved? what was damaged / lost? project / contract review what to check? documents to request suggested wording policy / certificate liability what policy applies? what to check? key policy questions suggested wording cause investigation what is the alleged cause? what to establish? documents / evidence suggested wording car warranties / conditions what to check? suggested wording theft / missing materials under car what to check? documents to request suggested wording temporary works / formwork / scaffolding what to check? suggested wording defective workmanship / design / material what to check? suggested wording third party liability under car what to check? documents to request suggested wording quantum / adjustment what to check? documents to request suggested wording application samples 1. missing formwork at project site facts application suggested wording 2. collapse of partially completed works facts application suggested wording 3. damage during concreting facts application suggested wording 4. theft of construction materials facts application suggested wording 5. damage to existing property facts application suggested wording 6. damage to third party property facts application suggested wording 7. loss during maintenance period facts application suggested wording 8. contractor’s plant and machinery damage facts application suggested wording 9. damage caused by subcontractor facts application suggested wording 10. claim within deductible facts application suggested wording report wording bank opening policy basis project verification cause pending warranty pending liability reserved quantum pending decline / doubtful wording recovery key reminder investigation contractors' all risks (car) claim — investigation aspect simple meaning car claim involves loss / damage connected to contract works during construction period. investigation must not only look at the physical damage. we must also check: project details contract period scope of works insured parties site location cause of loss whether damage involves contract works whether damage involves existing property whether damage involves temporary works whether damage involves contractor’s plant / equipment whether third party liability is involved whether car warranties / conditions were complied with whether any exclusion applies key point car is project based. always tie the loss back to: the contract the project site the insured works the stage of works the cause of damage the policy period / maintenance period the section of cover project / contract review what to check? contract title. contract value. contract period. commencement date. completion date. extension of time, if any. maintenance / defects liability period. scope of works. project site location. employer / principal. main contractor. subcontractor. party responsible for damaged works. whether damaged property forms part of contract works. documents to request letter of award. contract agreement. bill of quantities. scope of works. project programme. site layout plan. construction drawings. method statement. work permit. site diary. daily progress report. progress claim. completion certificate. extension of time approval. handover / cpc / practical completion certificate. maintenance / defects liability documents. suggested wording we have requested the contract documents, letter of award, scope of works, project programme and site records to verify whether the damaged property falls within the insured contract works and whether the loss occurred during the operative period of the car policy. policy / certificate liability what policy applies? contractors' all risks. car takaful. car material damage section. car third party liability section. contractors' plant and machinery section, if any. principal’s existing property extension, if any. maintenance cover / defects liability extension, if any. transit / offsite storage extension, if any. what to check? policy / certificate number. period of insurance. contract period. maintenance period. project description. risk location. insured parties. sum insured. contract value. limit for temporary works. limit for existing property. limit for third party liability. deductible / excess. endorsements. warranties. exclusions. whether the loss occurred at the insured project site. whether the damaged item is insured. key policy questions is the project named in the policy? is the risk location correct? is the date of loss within the policy period? has the project already been handed over? is the damaged property part of the contract works? is the damaged property existing property? is the damaged property temporary works? is the damaged property contractor’s plant and equipment? is the damaged property owned by subcontractor? is the loss under material damage or third party liability? is there any relevant extension? is there any exclusion? is there any breach of warranty? suggested wording the policy / certificate is noted to be a contractors' all risks cover, subject to the terms, exclusions, provisions and conditions contained therein or endorsed thereon. our comments on liability are subject to confirmation that the damaged property falls within the insured contract works and that the loss occurred within the operative policy / certificate period. cause investigation what is the alleged cause? theft. collapse. defective workmanship. defective design. defective material. negligence by contractor / subcontractor. impact by plant / machinery. fire. flood / rainwater. storm. landslip / subsidence. failure of temporary support. failure of formwork. overloading. poor supervision. non compliance with method statement. testing / commissioning failure. vandalism / malicious damage. what to establish? immediate cause. proximate cause. whether damage was sudden and accidental. whether damage was gradual / progressive. whether damage was due to defective work. whether damage was due to design error. whether damage was due to poor workmanship. whether damage was due to wear and tear. whether damage was due to normal settlement / shrinkage. whether damage was due to contractor’s negligence. whether loss is excluded or partly excluded. whether resultant damage is separable from defective part. documents / evidence site photographs. cctv footage. police report, if theft. incident report. site diary. consultant report. engineer’s report. method statement. inspection and test plan. material test result. concrete cube test. soil investigation report. weather record. work permit. ncr / non conformance report. qa/qc records. repair proposal. rectification method statement. suggested wording based on our preliminary investigation, the proximate cause of loss is still subject to review of site records, consultant findings and technical documents. we have requested the relevant method statement, drawings, site diary, incident report and engineer’s comments to establish the actual cause of damage. car warranties / conditions what to check? site security warranty. watchman / guard warranty. storage warranty. open storage warranty. fire prevention warranty. hot work permit requirement. dewatering / drainage requirement. temporary support / shoring requirement. testing / commissioning condition. maintenance period condition. theft prevention condition. material storage condition. compliance with statutory requirements. compliance with approved drawings / method statement. suggested wording the policy / certificate is noted to contain car warranties / conditions. we have requested site security records, method statements, work permits and site inspection records to verify compliance. pending receipt of the above, our comments on warranty compliance are reserved. theft / missing materials under car what to check? is theft covered under car? is there full theft clause / theft extension? was there forcible entry? was the material kept at insured site? was it stored in open yard? was the area fenced / guarded? was there cctv? was police report lodged? was stock / material record available? was the missing material already incorporated into the works? was it loose material? was it temporary works? was it contractor’s plant / tools? was it excluded? documents to request police report. site security report. guard duty roster. cctv footage. delivery order. purchase invoice. material issue record. stock card. site inventory. photos of storage area. contract document showing material included in works. suggested wording the claim involves alleged theft / missing materials from the project site. we have requested police report, site security records, cctv footage, delivery orders and material inventory records to verify the occurrence, ownership and quantum of the missing items. policy liability is subject to confirmation that theft is covered under the car policy and that the relevant site security conditions / warranties were complied with. temporary works / formwork / scaffolding what to check? was the damaged / missing item temporary works? is temporary works included in sum insured? was formwork / scaffolding owned by contractor or rented? was it part of contract value? was it reusable contractor’s equipment? was it located at project site? was there inventory record? was it damaged or merely missing? was there theft evidence? was there any exclusion for tools / equipment / temporary works? was there recovery from subcontractor / supplier? suggested wording the claim concerns formwork / temporary works at the project site. we have requested supporting documents to confirm ownership, value, location, usage and whether the item forms part of the insured contract works or contractor’s temporary equipment. policy liability is subject to the applicable car wording, insured property description and any exclusion relating to temporary works / contractor’s plant and equipment. defective workmanship / design / material what to check? was the damage caused by defective workmanship? was it defective design? was it defective material? is only the defective part damaged? is there resultant damage to other insured property? does the policy exclude cost of rectifying defective work? does any de / leg endorsement apply? was consultant / engineer involved? was subcontractor responsible? is recovery possible? suggested wording the available evidence suggests that the damage may be connected to defective workmanship / design / material. we recommend insurers / operators review the applicable exclusion / defect endorsement. the cost of rectifying the defective part may require separate consideration from any resultant physical damage to other insured property. third party liability under car what to check? was there damage to third party property? was there bodily injury? was claimant a true third party? is claimant also an insured party? does cross liability clause apply? was negligence established? was damage caused by contract works? is there any exclusion for vibration / removal / weakening of support? was there any underground services damage? was there prior condition of third party property? was notice of claim received? documents to request third party claim letter. photos of third party damage. police / authority report, if any. incident report. witness statement. pre condition survey. joint inspection record. repair quotation. proof of ownership. consultant / engineer report. suggested wording the claim involves potential third party liability arising from contract works. we have requested third party claim documents, photographs, incident report, pre condition survey and supporting repair quotation. liability is subject to proof of negligence, policy terms, exclusions and any applicable cross liability clause. quantum / adjustment what to check? what is damaged? what repair is necessary? is repair scope reasonable? is replacement necessary? is there betterment? is there salvage? is there any duplicate claim? is cost part of original contract obligation? is rectification of defective work excluded? is temporary repair included? is mitigation cost reasonable? is loss within deductible? is there contribution from subcontractor / supplier / warranty? is there recovery / subrogation potential? documents to request repair quotation. rectification proposal. contractor’s breakdown. consultant’s certification. boq rates. original contract rates. purchase invoices. delivery orders. payment records. labour and material breakdown. photos before and after repair. salvage details. subcontractor backcharge records. suggested wording our adjustment is based on the reasonable cost of reinstating the damaged insured property to its pre loss condition, subject to policy terms, exclusions, deductible and verification of supporting documents. any cost relating to improvement, betterment, defective work rectification or unrelated contract obligation has been excluded / reserved for further review. application samples 1. missing formwork at project site facts formwork reported missing from construction site. car policy in force. need to confirm if formwork is insured property or contractor’s equipment. need to confirm theft evidence and storage condition. application do not immediately treat as payable car loss. check whether formwork is part of contract works, temporary works or contractor’s plant / equipment. check whether theft / disappearance is covered. check police report, site security and inventory. suggested wording the claim concerns missing formwork from the project site. we have requested police report, site security records, inventory records, delivery orders and supporting invoices to verify the occurrence and value of the alleged missing formwork. policy liability is subject to confirmation that the formwork falls within the insured property under the car policy and that theft / disappearance is covered under the applicable wording. 2. collapse of partially completed works facts part of retaining wall / slab / structure collapsed. works not yet completed. cause uncertain. application check design, workmanship, material, soil condition, temporary support and method statement. separate defective part from resultant damage. suggested wording the damaged property forms part of the contract works under construction. the cause of collapse remains subject to review of drawings, method statement, consultant’s report, site records and material test results. policy liability is reserved pending confirmation whether the damage was sudden and accidental or arose from defective design / workmanship / material. 3. damage during concreting facts formwork failed during concreting. concrete spilled / slab damaged. works require demolition and re casting. application check formwork design, temporary support, pouring sequence, supervision and method statement. check whether defective formwork cost is excluded. consider resultant damage. suggested wording the loss occurred during concreting works. we have requested method statement, formwork design, site supervision records, pour record and consultant’s comments to determine the proximate cause. any cost relating to defective temporary works / formwork is subject to policy exclusion, while resultant damage to insured contract works may require separate consideration. 4. theft of construction materials facts materials stored at site were stolen. materials not yet incorporated into works. site fenced and guarded. application check material ownership and whether materials are insured before incorporation. check security warranty. check police report and inventory. suggested wording the stolen materials were reportedly stored at the insured project site but had not yet been incorporated into the works. we have requested delivery orders, purchase invoices, material stock records, police report and guard records. policy liability is subject to confirmation that materials stored at site are covered and that applicable security warranties were complied with. 5. damage to existing property facts renovation works damaged existing building. existing property belongs to principal. car policy covers contract works but existing property extension uncertain. application check whether principal’s existing property extension is endorsed. if not endorsed, liability may be doubtful. suggested wording the damaged property appears to be existing property and not part of the contract works. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether principal’s existing property extension is endorsed and whether the applicable limit is sufficient. pending confirmation, policy liability is reserved. 6. damage to third party property facts construction works caused cracks to neighbour’s building. third party submitted claim. pre condition survey may exist. application check negligence, causation and policy tpl section. check exclusion for vibration / weakening of support. suggested wording the claim involves alleged third party property damage arising from contract works. we have requested pre condition survey, third party repair quotation, photographs, incident report and consultant’s comments. liability is subject to proof of negligence, causation, applicable car third party liability section and policy exclusions. 7. loss during maintenance period facts defect / damage discovered after practical completion. works already handed over. policy has maintenance cover. application check whether loss occurred during maintenance period. check type of maintenance cover. check whether damage arose from work carried out during construction or maintenance visit. suggested wording the loss was discovered after practical completion / handover. we recommend insurers / operators confirm the operative maintenance period and scope of maintenance cover. liability is subject to whether the damage falls within the maintenance extension and policy conditions. 8. contractor’s plant and machinery damage facts excavator / crane / generator damaged at site. car policy in force. plant may not be included under contract works. application check whether contractor’s plant and machinery section / extension exists. otherwise may need cpm policy. suggested wording the damaged item appears to be contractor’s plant / machinery and not part of the permanent contract works. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the car policy includes contractors’ plant and machinery cover. if not, the claim may need to be considered under a separate cpm policy, if available. 9. damage caused by subcontractor facts subcontractor caused damage to contract works. subcontractor may be named insured. application check insured parties and cross liability. consider recovery if subcontractor not insured or contract allows backcharge. suggested wording the damage appears to have arisen from works carried out by subcontractor. we have requested the subcontract agreement and insured party schedule to confirm whether the subcontractor is covered under the car policy. recovery / backcharge potential should be considered depending on contract terms and policy position. 10. claim within deductible facts adjusted loss is below deductible. policy technically responds but no net payable amount. application state liability subject to deductible. no payment if below deductible. suggested wording the adjusted loss is below the applicable deductible under the car policy. accordingly, there is no net amount payable by insurers / operators, subject to their confirmation of policy liability. report wording bank opening we were instructed to investigate a contractors’ all risks claim arising from loss / damage at the project site. our investigation focused on circumstances, cause, project details, policy liability, warranty compliance and quantum. policy basis the policy / certificate is noted to be a contractors’ all risks cover, subject to terms, exclusions, provisions and conditions contained therein or endorsed thereon. project verification we have requested contract documents to verify the project description, contract value, insured parties, project period and whether the damaged property forms part of insured contract works. cause pending the proximate cause remains subject to review of site records, method statement, consultant’s report and technical documents. warranty pending compliance with car warranties / conditions cannot be confirmed at this stage pending receipt of site security records, method statements, work permits and site inspection records. liability reserved policy liability is reserved pending confirmation of policy wording, insured property description, operative period, cause of loss and applicable exclusions. quantum pending our comments on quantum are reserved pending receipt of repair breakdown, boq rates, supporting invoices, consultant certification and photographs. decline / doubtful wording based on the documents presently available, the damaged property does not appear to fall within the insured contract works / covered section. policy liability appears doubtful, subject to insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. recovery recovery / backcharge against subcontractor / supplier / responsible party should be considered depending on contract terms and investigation findings. key reminder car claim is not just “damage at site”. always confirm: is the project insured? is the location correct? is the date within policy / maintenance period? is damaged property part of contract works? is it existing property? is it temporary works? is it contractor’s plant? is it third party property? what caused the loss? is cause excluded? were warranties complied with? is deductible applicable? endorsement clauses might applies: 1. cross liability clause 2. principal’s existing property clause or existing property extension 3. maintenance period clause or defects liability clause 4. testing and commissioning clause 5. temporary work clause 6. contractors’ plant and machinery clause or cpm extension 7. offsite storage clause 8. inland transit clause 9. vibration, removal, weakening of support clause 10. design, defective workmanship, defective material clause 11. escalation clause

documents.title: Contractors’ Plant and Machinery Clause or CPM Extension
documents.relativePath: Contractors’ Plant and Machinery Clause or CPM Extension.md
documents.excerpt: Covers contractor’s plant, machinery and equipment only if specifically included.

headings.text: Crucial Application
What to check
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: contractors’ plant and machinery clause or cpm extension contractors’ plant and machinery clause or cpm extension.md policy & clauses crucial application what to check suggested wording endorsement clauses car contractor all risk meaning covers contractor’s plant, machinery and equipment only if specifically included. car contract works cover usually does not automatically cover contractor’s plant. excavator, crane, generator, compressor and tools may need separate cpm cover. crucial application apply when damage involves equipment used to perform the work, not the work itself. example: excavator damaged at site. crane overturned. generator stolen. contractor’s tools damaged. what to check is cpm extension included? is the item listed / scheduled? is it contractor’s plant or part of permanent works? was it at insured site? was it operating at time of loss? is machinery breakdown excluded? is theft covered? is there separate cpm policy? suggested wording the damaged item appears to be contractor’s plant / machinery and not part of the permanent contract works. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether contractors’ plant and machinery cover is included under the car policy. if not, the claim may need to be considered under a separate cpm policy, if available.

documents.relativePath: CPI Clause or Consumer Price Index Clause.md
documents.excerpt: CPI Clause adjusts Sum Insured / Sum Covered based on consumer price index.

headings.text: Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: cpi clause or consumer price index clause cpi clause or consumer price index clause.md policy & clauses key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses simple meaning cpi clause adjusts sum insured / sum covered based on consumer price index. common in long term houseowner / building policies. used to update insured value over time. key point check original sum insured and cpi index at inception vs date of loss / current year. application sample facts long term houseowner certificate. cpi clause attached. sum covered at inception rm299,000. need updated sum covered. suggested wording the certificate is noted to be subject to cpi clause. the sum covered should be reviewed based on the applicable cpi index. we recommend insurers / operators confirm the adjusted sum covered before finalising adequacy and settlement.

documents.title: Cross Liability Clause
documents.excerpt: Cross Liability Clause treats each insured party as if separate policies were issued to each party.

headings.text: Simple Meaning
Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: cross liability clause cross liability clause.md policy & clauses simple meaning key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses 4. cross liability clause simple meaning cross liability clause treats each insured party as if separate policies were issued to each party. common in public liability / car / project policies with multiple insured parties. it may allow one insured party to claim against another insured party under liability section. key point important where more than one insured party is involved. check who caused damage and who suffered damage. application sample facts main contractor and subcontractor are both named insured. subcontractor’s work caused damage to principal’s / main contractor’s property. liability issue arises between insured parties. suggested wording the policy is noted to include cross liability clause. as more than one insured party is involved, we recommend insurers / operators consider the claim as though separate policies had been issued to each insured party, subject to the terms and exclusions of the policy. liability remains subject to proof of negligence, insured status, policy section and applicable exclusions.

documents.title: Design, Defective workmanship, Defective Material Clause
documents.relativePath: Design, Defective workmanship, Defective Material Clause.md
documents.excerpt: CAR policies normally exclude cost of rectifying defective design, workmanship or material.

headings.text: Crucial Application
What to check
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: design, defective workmanship, defective material clause design, defective workmanship, defective material clause.md policy & clauses crucial application what to check suggested wording endorsement clauses car contractor all risk meaning car policies normally exclude cost of rectifying defective design, workmanship or material. some endorsements may give wider cover for resultant damage. need to separate defective part from resultant damage. crucial application apply when damage arises from design error, poor workmanship, wrong material, wrong method or defective temporary works. example: formwork fails during concreting. waterproofing wrongly installed. retaining wall collapses due to design issue. defective weld causes collapse. what to check what is the defective part? is there resultant damage? does policy have de / leg endorsement? is cost claimed rectification of defect or damage caused by defect? was consultant / engineer responsible? was subcontractor responsible? any recovery / backcharge? suggested wording the available evidence suggests possible defective design / workmanship / material. we recommend insurers / operators review the applicable defect exclusion / endorsement. the cost of rectifying the defective part should be considered separately from any resultant physical damage to other insured property.

documents.excerpt: If fire starts from the machine / electrical component and the fire damages the machine itself, damage to the machine may be payable.

documents.searchText: electrical clause b electrical clause b.md policy & clauses simple meaning key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses 2. electrical clause b simple meaning electrical clause b is wider than clause a. if fire starts from the machine / electrical component and the fire damages the machine itself, damage to the machine may be payable. resultant fire damage to other property may also be payable. key point clause b may allow the originating machine damage if actual fire occurred and damaged the machine. application sample facts fire started from electrical component inside machine. fire damaged the machine itself. fire also spread to nearby property. suggested wording the fire appears to have originated from the machine / electrical component. as the policy is noted to be subject to electrical clause b, the fire damage to the affected machine may be considered. resultant fire damage to surrounding property is also considered subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions and excess.

documents.searchText: first loss without average clause — claim notes first loss without average clause.md policy & clauses first loss without average clause — claim notes simple meaning easy way to remember key point when this clause is usually used adjuster review scope circumstances policy / certificate liability quantum formula if adjusted loss is below first loss limit if adjusted loss exceeds first loss limit average application samples 1. burglary claim below first loss limit facts application suggested wording 2. burglary claim exceeds first loss limit facts application suggested wording 3. stock loss where total stock value is much higher facts application suggested wording 4. money loss first loss basis facts application suggested wording 5. loss exceeds first loss limit but insured claims full amount facts application suggested wording 6. first loss clause endorsed but item not covered facts application suggested wording 7. first loss clause endorsed but cause excluded facts application suggested wording 8. first loss clause with burglary but no forcible entry facts application suggested wording 9. first loss clause with full theft extension facts application suggested wording 10. first loss limit applies per location facts application suggested wording 11. first loss limit applies in aggregate facts application suggested wording report wording bank clause sighted no average applied adjusted loss within limit adjusted loss exceeds limit total value at risk not reviewed clause does not confirm liability protective wording short wording common mistakes mistake 1 correct mistake 2 correct mistake 3 correct mistake 4 correct key point endorsement clauses first loss without average clause — claim notes simple meaning first loss without average clause means the policy / certificate is arranged on first loss basis. the sum insured / limit represents the maximum amount payable for one loss. the insurer does not require the insured to insure the full total value at risk. average / underinsurance is not applied. settlement is still subject to: policy terms conditions exclusions limits excess proof of loss reasonable adjustment easy way to remember normal policy: sum insured should represent full value at risk. if underinsured, average may apply. first loss without average: sum insured is a selected first loss limit. full value at risk is not used to reduce the claim. average is waived / not applicable. claim is payable up to the first loss limit. key point first loss without average does not mean automatic full payment. it only means average is not applied. the loss must still be covered, proven and adjusted. the payable amount cannot exceed the first loss limit. adjuster review scope circumstances what happened? fire / theft / burglary / money loss / accidental damage / flood / impact. what property was lost or damaged? stock contents equipment cash machinery fixtures and fittings other insured property what is the insured section? fire burglary money all risks fidelity guarantee special perils engineering what is the first loss limit? rm[amount] any one loss rm[amount] any one occurrence rm[amount] any one period rm[amount] aggregate, if applicable policy / certificate liability is first loss without average clause endorsed? which section does it apply to? what is the first loss limit? is the damaged / stolen property insured? is the cause of loss covered? is there any exclusion? is there any excess? is there any sub limit? is there any warranty / condition? is there any aggregate limit? is average clearly waived? is the loss within the first loss limit? quantum adjust the actual loss first. check supporting documents. remove unrelated / unsupported items. apply depreciation / betterment if policy basis requires. apply excess. compare adjusted loss with first loss limit. pay the lower of: adjusted loss; or first loss limit. formula if adjusted loss is below first loss limit payable = adjusted loss excess if adjusted loss exceeds first loss limit payable = first loss limit excess average no average applied if the clause clearly states “without average”. application samples 1. burglary claim below first loss limit facts burglary claim. first loss without average limit: rm50,000. adjusted stolen stock: rm32,000. excess: rm500. application adjusted loss is below first loss limit. no average applied. pay adjusted loss less excess. suggested wording the policy is noted to be arranged on first loss without average basis under the burglary section. the adjusted loss amounts to rm32,000.00, which is below the first loss limit of rm50,000.00. accordingly, no average is applied. the recommended settlement is rm31,500.00 after deduction of excess. 2. burglary claim exceeds first loss limit facts burglary claim. first loss limit: rm50,000. adjusted loss: rm78,000. excess: rm500. application adjusted loss exceeds first loss limit. payable is capped at first loss limit. no average applied. suggested wording the adjusted loss amounts to rm78,000.00. however, the policy is subject to first loss without average limit of rm50,000.00. accordingly, our recommendation is restricted to the first loss limit, less applicable excess. no average is applied in view of the first loss without average clause. 3. stock loss where total stock value is much higher facts total stock value at risk: rm500,000. first loss limit: rm100,000. adjusted stock loss: rm60,000. insured did not insure full stock value. application do not apply average based on rm500,000 value at risk. first loss arrangement accepts that limit is less than full value. pay adjusted loss if within limit. suggested wording although the total stock value at risk exceeds the first loss limit, the policy is arranged on first loss without average basis. we have therefore not applied average / underinsurance. the adjusted stock loss of rm60,000.00 falls within the first loss limit and is recommended accordingly, subject to policy terms and excess. 4. money loss first loss basis facts money stolen from premises. first loss limit: rm10,000. proven money loss: rm7,500. excess: rm250. application check money section conditions. no average issue. pay proven amount less excess. suggested wording the money section is noted to be covered on first loss without average basis. the proven loss of money amounts to rm7,500.00, which is within the first loss limit. we recommend settlement of rm7,250.00 after deduction of excess. 5. loss exceeds first loss limit but insured claims full amount facts adjusted loss: rm150,000. first loss limit: rm100,000. insured claims full rm150,000. application explain cap clearly. no average, but limit still applies. suggested wording the claim has been adjusted at rm150,000.00. however, the policy limit under the first loss without average clause is rm100,000.00. the clause waives average but does not increase the policy limit. accordingly, the maximum payable amount is restricted to rm100,000.00, subject to excess and other policy conditions. 6. first loss clause endorsed but item not covered facts first loss without average clause endorsed. claimed item not insured under the section. loss amount is within limit. application clause does not create cover for uninsured items. liability still doubtful / not payable. suggested wording although the policy is arranged on first loss without average basis, the clause only affects the application of average. it does not extend cover to items not insured under the policy. as the claimed item does not fall within the insured property description, we are unable to recommend settlement for this item. 7. first loss clause endorsed but cause excluded facts loss within first loss limit. cause falls under exclusion. application clause does not override exclusions. suggested wording the adjusted loss falls within the first loss limit. however, the cause of loss appears to fall within the policy exclusion. the first loss without average clause does not override the exclusions under the policy. policy liability remains subject to insurers’ / operators’ final consideration. 8. first loss clause with burglary but no forcible entry facts burglary section has first loss limit. no evidence of forcible and violent entry. missing stock reported. application first loss clause does not remove burglary requirement. if standard burglary wording applies, liability remains doubtful. suggested wording the burglary section is noted to be on first loss without average basis. however, the clause only affects the application of average and does not remove the requirement to establish burglary as defined under the policy. in the absence of evidence of forcible and violent entry, policy liability remains doubtful, subject to insurers’ / operators’ final decision. 9. first loss clause with full theft extension facts full theft clause endorsed. first loss without average limit: rm30,000. outdoor compressor stolen. adjusted loss: rm18,000. application consider full theft wording first. then apply first loss limit. no average. suggested wording the policy is noted to include full theft clause and first loss without average basis. subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation that the full theft clause applies to the stolen item, the adjusted loss of rm18,000.00 falls within the first loss limit. no average is applied. 10. first loss limit applies per location facts multiple locations insured. first loss limit applies per location. loss occurred at one branch. application check schedule carefully. do not combine limits unless wording allows. suggested wording the first loss limit appears to apply per location as stated in the schedule. the loss occurred at [location]. our recommendation is therefore restricted to the first loss limit applicable to that location, subject to policy terms and excess. 11. first loss limit applies in aggregate facts first loss limit is aggregate for period. earlier claim already paid. new claim occurs. application deduct previous payment from aggregate limit. check remaining available balance. suggested wording the first loss without average limit is subject to aggregate limit for the period of insurance. we recommend insurers / operators confirm previous claims paid under this section. the present settlement should be restricted to the remaining available balance, if any. report wording bank clause sighted the policy / certificate is noted to be endorsed with first loss without average clause. no average applied in view of the first loss without average basis, we have not applied average / underinsurance. adjusted loss within limit the adjusted loss falls within the first loss limit and is recommended subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions and excess. adjusted loss exceeds limit the adjusted loss exceeds the first loss limit. our recommendation is therefore restricted to the first loss limit, less applicable excess. total value at risk not reviewed as the policy is arranged on first loss without average basis, we have not reviewed the adequacy of sum insured against total value at risk for the purpose of applying average. clause does not confirm liability the first loss without average clause only affects the application of average. it does not determine policy liability or override exclusions, warranties, limits or proof of loss requirements. protective wording our recommendation is subject to the exact wording of the first loss without average clause, applicable limits, policy terms, exclusions, warranties and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. short wording the policy is on first loss without average basis. accordingly, no average has been applied and settlement is restricted to the first loss limit. common mistakes mistake 1 thinking first loss means full claim is payable. correct first loss only gives a limit. payable amount is still subject to adjustment and policy liability. mistake 2 applying average despite “without average”. correct if the clause clearly says without average, do not apply underinsurance / average. mistake 3 ignoring the first loss limit. correct even without average, the payable amount cannot exceed the first loss limit. mistake 4 treating the clause as a cover extension. correct it is not a cover extension. it does not make excluded property payable. key point first loss without average = no average, but capped by first loss limit. still check liability. still check insured property. still check cause of loss. still check documents. still check excess. still check sub limits / aggregate limits. do not check full value at risk for average unless wording requires it.

effects of floods prevention & safety measures terms & concepts investigation flood claim — adjuster investigation / review scope confirm basic claim details date and time of loss location / risk premises insured / participant name policy / certificate number period of insurance type of premises usage of premises amount claimed date claim was notified establish alleged cause of loss flood flash flood overflow of river / drain / monsoon drain surface runoff heavy rain flood with windstorm backflow from drainage system water ingress due to rising flood water interview insured / participant ask when flood water started entering the premises ask the maximum flood water level ask how long the water remained ask whether the area was generally flooded ask whether neighbouring premises were also affected ask whether electricity was shut down ask whether emergency cleaning or drying was carried out ask whether damaged items were disposed of before inspection inspect the risk premises check flood water mark on wall, door, gate, furniture, stock, or machinery measure approximate flood water height take photos of water marks and affected areas check entry point of flood water check surrounding drains, road level, river, slope, or low lying area confirm whether damage is consistent with flood water ingress separate flood damage from old damage or wear and tear inspect damaged property building electrical installation stock in trade plant and machinery furniture and fixtures office equipment household contents renovation / improvement works documents / records, if claimed verify extent of flood damage identify items actually affected by flood water check whether items were submerged or only exposed to moisture check whether items can be cleaned, repaired, serviced, or salvaged confirm whether replacement is necessary check for mud stains, rust, swelling, water marks, corrosion, short circuit, or contamination check flood level and severity record water level in feet / inches / centimetres record duration of inundation record affected floor level record whether ground floor, basement, store, factory, warehouse, or office was affected in one flood report, the premises was inundated with flood water reaching about 3 feet, affecting building, electrical installation, stock, plant and machinery, furniture and fixtures source reference: finalreport2296 2021 recorded flood and windstorm, with flood water reaching 3 feet review policy / certificate coverage check whether flood is covered check whether flood extension is endorsed check whether the policy is fire, commercial fire, houseowner, householder, all risks, or other class check sum insured check sub limits check excess / deductible check average clause / underinsurance check basis of settlement check exclusions and warranties check whether contents, stock, machinery, and building are separately insured important coverage point do not assume flood is automatically covered under a basic fire policy confirm whether flood / special perils extension is included confirm whether the damaged property falls under the insured section confirm whether the loss occurred during the policy period confirm whether the claim is subject to special excess or flood deductible request supporting documents completed claim form policy / certificate schedule photos of flood and damaged property repair quotation replacement quotation contractor report purchase invoices asset listing stock listing inventory records audited accounts / stock records, if stock claim involved machinery service report, if machinery involved cleaning / drying invoices salvage details police report / authority report, if required by insurer assess building damage identify flood damaged building components wall floor door ceiling electrical wiring distribution board painting built in cabinet renovation works confirm whether repair scope is reasonable separate flood related repairs from improvement or betterment assess stock damage obtain stock listing before and after loss check stock location at time of flood check whether stock was stored on floor, rack, pallet, or upper level identify damaged quantity identify salvageable quantity check cost price, selling price, and supporting invoices apply stock valuation basis under policy deduct salvage value where applicable assess plant and machinery damage identify machinery affected by flood water check water level against machinery components obtain technician / service report confirm whether cleaning, servicing, rewinding, repair, or replacement is required check age and condition apply depreciation or betterment if required consider whether damage is due to flood or pre existing mechanical/electrical defect assess contents / furniture / fixtures identify items directly affected by flood check age, condition, and ownership check whether items can be cleaned or repaired apply depreciation where settlement is on indemnity basis exclude unsupported or unrelated items consider mitigation check whether insured took reasonable steps to reduce loss pump out flood water clean mud and debris dry affected area move stock / contents to higher ground arrange emergency electrical inspection prevent further damage keep receipts for emergency works consider salvage identify items with salvage value separate total loss items from repairable items record disposal method obtain salvage offer if necessary deduct salvage value from assessment where applicable assess quantum compare claim amount with supporting documents verify repair and replacement costs apply policy limits and sub limits apply depreciation / betterment apply average clause if underinsured apply excess / deductible deduct salvage recommend net adjusted amount payment / settlement consideration consider payment on account for large losses, subject to insurer approval in one commercial flood report, the claim amount was rm2,863,583.05, adjusted to rm1,133,234.17 net of excess, with rm200,000.00 payment on account and balance amount due recorded source reference: finalreport2296 2021 flood and windstorm claim prepare report to insurer / operator circumstances of loss cause of loss flood level and duration inspection findings nature and extent of damage coverage position documents reviewed quantum assessment salvage position excess / deductible recommendation on liability recommendation on settlement key point for flood claim, confirm that flood actually occurred and directly caused the damage verify flood level, affected items, and supporting documents check whether flood / special perils extension is covered separate flood related damage from pre existing damage, wear and tear, and unsupported items can you make all the above in keypoints? with readable heading etc i.e. meanings, terms etc certainly! here are the keypoints based on the title "flood" with structured headings: flood definition a flood is an overflow of water that submerges land which is usually dry. causes of floods heavy rainfall over a short period. melting snow and ice. storm surges from cyclones or hurricanes. dam or levee failures. rapid urbanization leading to poor drainage. types of floods river floods: occur when rivers overflow their banks due to excessive rainfall. urban floods: result from inadequate drainage systems in cities. coastal floods: caused by storm surges and high tides during storms or cyclones. flash floods: sudden floods typically caused by intense rainfall over a small area. effects of floods loss of lives and injuries. destruction of homes, infrastructure, and crops. spread of waterborne diseases. economic losses due to damage and disruption. prevention & safety measures building dams and levees for flood control. proper urban planning with effective drainage systems. early warning systems for flood alerts. evacuation plans and emergency preparedness. terms & concepts floodplain: the flat area around a river prone to flooding. levee/dam: structures built to prevent flooding or control water flow. flood warning: alerts issued to inform about impending floods. let me know if you'd like more detailed explanations on any section!

documents.title: Full Theft Clause / Full Theft Extension
documents.excerpt: Full Theft Clause extends cover beyond standard burglary wording.

headings.text: Full Theft Clause / Full Theft Extension
Meaning
Key Difference
Important
Report Wording Bank
Full Theft Clause sighted
Outdoor item within compound
No forcible entry but full theft applies
Liability reserved
Full Theft Clause not sighted
Recommended conclusion

documents.searchText: full theft clause / full theft extension full theft.md policy & clauses full theft clause / full theft extension meaning key difference important report wording bank full theft clause sighted outdoor item within compound no forcible entry but full theft applies liability reserved full theft clause not sighted recommended conclusion burglary. full theft clause / full theft extension meaning full theft clause extends cover beyond standard burglary wording. standard burglary usually requires actual forcible and violent entry into or exit from the premises. full theft clause may cover theft even where there is no forcible and violent entry, subject to the policy wording. it is commonly relevant where the item itself is stolen from the premises / compound / open area. key difference burglary: requires forcible and violent entry, unless wording says otherwise. full theft: may cover theft of the insured property without needing to prove forcible and violent entry. still subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions, limits, excess and warranties. important do not assume full theft clause covers everything. always check: is full theft clause endorsed? what property is covered? is there any limit? is property in the open covered? is the item within the insured premises / compound? is the item permanently installed? is there any exclusion for unattended property? is there any security warranty? was police report lodged? is ownership proven? report wording bank full theft clause sighted the policy is noted to be endorsed with full theft clause. accordingly, the claim may be considered under the said clause, subject to its terms, conditions, limits and exclusions. outdoor item within compound the stolen item was located outdoors but within the insured compound at the material time. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the full theft clause extends to property situated within the open compound. no forcible entry but full theft applies although there was no evidence of forcible and violent entry, the policy is noted to include full theft clause. we therefore considered the claim under the full theft clause instead of standard burglary wording. liability reserved liability is reserved pending confirmation of the exact scope of the full theft clause and whether the stolen item falls within the insured property description. full theft clause not sighted we did not sight any full theft clause / theft extension in the policy schedule. the claim is therefore considered under the standard burglary wording, which requires evidence of actual forcible and violent entry. recommended conclusion subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation that the full theft clause applies to property situated within the compound, the claim may be considered under the policy.

documents.searchText: full value clause — claim notes full value clause.md policy & clauses full value clause — claim notes simple meaning easy way to remember key point adjuster review scope circumstances policy / certificate liability documents to request value at risk meaning example formula example calculation application samples 1. full value clause — sum insured adequate facts application suggested wording 2. full value clause — sum insured inadequate facts application suggested wording 3. full value clause — loss exceeds sum insured facts application suggested wording 4. full value clause with reinstatement value facts application suggested wording 5. full value clause with market value facts application suggested wording 6. full value clause but appraisement clause applies facts application suggested wording 7. full value clause but first loss without average applies facts application suggested wording 8. full value clause — stock records incomplete facts application suggested wording 9. full value clause — machinery and equipment facts application suggested wording 10. full value clause — building valuation pending facts application suggested wording report wording bank clause sighted adequacy reviewed sum insured adequate sum insured inadequate adequacy pending average reserved other clause overrides policy limit protective wording common mistakes mistake 1 correct mistake 2 correct mistake 3 correct mistake 4 correct key point endorsement clauses full value clause — claim notes simple meaning full value clause means the insured property should be insured for its full value. the sum insured should represent the full value at risk. it is not a first loss limit. it is not a waiver of average. if the sum insured is lower than the actual value at risk, average / underinsurance may apply. easy way to remember full value clause: insure the whole value. check adequacy of sum insured. average may apply if underinsured. first loss without average: insure selected first loss amount only. no average if clearly stated. payable capped by first loss limit. appraisement clause: small loss below stated limit. no need to check adequacy of sum insured. average not reviewed because loss is small. key point full value clause does not mean full claim amount is payable. it means the sum insured should be compared against the full value at risk. if sum insured is adequate, no average. if sum insured is inadequate, apply average unless waived by another clause. claim is still subject to: policy terms conditions exclusions excess limits basis of settlement proof of loss reasonable adjustment value at risk meaning value at risk is the actual full value of the insured property at the time of loss. it is the figure used to check whether the sum insured is adequate. example sum insured: rm500,000 value at risk: rm1,000,000 insured only insured 50% of the actual value. if average applies, only 50% of the adjusted loss is payable. formula payable loss = adjusted loss x sum insured / value at risk example calculation adjusted loss: rm100,000 sum insured: rm500,000 value at risk: rm1,000,000 rm100,000 x rm500,000 / rm1,000,000 payable before excess = rm50,000 application samples 1. full value clause — sum insured adequate facts building sum insured: rm1,000,000 building value at risk: rm950,000 adjusted repair cost: rm80,000 excess: rm1,000 application sum insured is adequate. no average applied. adjusted loss payable subject to excess. suggested wording the policy is noted to be subject to full value clause. we have reviewed the adequacy of the sum insured against the assessed value at risk. the sum insured of rm1,000,000.00 appears adequate against the assessed value at risk of rm950,000.00. accordingly, no average is applied. the adjusted loss is recommended subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions and excess. 2. full value clause — sum insured inadequate facts stock sum insured: rm300,000 stock value at risk: rm600,000 adjusted stock loss: rm120,000 excess: rm1,000 application sum insured is only 50% of value at risk. average applies unless waived. payable before excess = rm60,000. suggested wording the policy is noted to be subject to full value clause. our review indicates that the sum insured for stock is inadequate. the sum insured is rm300,000.00 against the assessed value at risk of rm600,000.00. accordingly, average is applied to the adjusted stock loss. the adjusted loss of rm120,000.00 is reduced proportionately to rm60,000.00 before application of excess. 3. full value clause — loss exceeds sum insured facts sum insured: rm100,000 value at risk: rm100,000 adjusted loss: rm150,000 application sum insured may be adequate at policy level. but claim cannot exceed policy limit. payable is capped at sum insured. suggested wording the adjusted loss exceeds the sum insured. although average is not applicable, the policy limit remains rm100,000.00. our recommendation is therefore restricted to the sum insured, subject to policy terms, conditions and excess. 4. full value clause with reinstatement value facts building covered on reinstatement value basis. full reinstatement value at risk: rm2,000,000 sum insured: rm1,500,000 adjusted repair cost: rm200,000 application for rvc, sum insured should represent full reinstatement value. if sum insured is inadequate, average may apply. full value clause reinforces adequacy review. suggested wording the policy is subject to reinstatement value basis and full value clause. the sum insured should represent the full reinstatement value of the insured property. our assessment indicates that the sum insured is inadequate against the reinstatement value at risk. accordingly, average is applied to the adjusted reinstatement loss, subject to policy terms and conditions. 5. full value clause with market value facts contents covered on market value basis. market value at risk: rm700,000 sum insured: rm500,000 adjusted indemnity loss: rm70,000 application sum insured should represent full market value. if underinsured, average may apply. suggested wording the policy is subject to market value basis and full value clause. the sum insured should represent the full market value of the insured property. as the sum insured is lower than the assessed market value at risk, average is applicable. the adjusted loss is reduced proportionately before application of excess. 6. full value clause but appraisement clause applies facts contents loss: rm3,800 appraisement clause limit: rm5,000 full value clause also endorsed. application if appraisement clause applies, adequacy of sum insured may not be reviewed for small loss. full value review not necessary if loss is below appraisement limit. suggested wording the policy is noted to be endorsed with appraisement clause. as the adjusted loss is below rm5,000.00, we have not reviewed adequacy of sum insured / value at risk. the claim is adjusted based on the verified loss, subject to policy terms and excess. 7. full value clause but first loss without average applies facts burglary section arranged on first loss without average basis. first loss limit: rm50,000 total stock value: rm500,000 adjusted burglary loss: rm40,000 application first loss without average controls that section. do not apply full value average if clause clearly waives average. pay subject to first loss limit. suggested wording although the total stock value at risk exceeds the first loss limit, the burglary section is arranged on first loss without average basis. accordingly, average is not applied. the adjusted loss falls within the first loss limit and is recommended subject to policy terms, conditions and excess. 8. full value clause — stock records incomplete facts stock claim submitted. need to verify value at risk. insured unable to provide full stock listing. application cannot confirm adequacy. request documents. reserve average position. suggested wording as the policy is subject to full value clause, we are required to review adequacy of sum insured against the full stock value at risk. we have requested complete stock listing, inventory records, purchase records and sales records. pending receipt of the above, our comments on adequacy of sum insured and average are reserved. 9. full value clause — machinery and equipment facts machinery sum insured: rm800,000 full replacement / reinstatement value: rm1,200,000 adjusted machinery repair cost: rm240,000 application underinsured. average may apply unless policy says otherwise. need valuation / supplier quotation. suggested wording the machinery section is subject to full value clause. based on available information, the sum insured appears inadequate against the full value of the machinery insured. we recommend applying average to the adjusted machinery loss, subject to insurers’ / operators’ confirmation of the value at risk. 10. full value clause — building valuation pending facts building loss. no valuation report yet. need reinstatement value / market value depending policy basis. application reserve adequacy comment. do not conclude average until valuation available. suggested wording the policy is subject to full value clause. we are arranging / awaiting assessment of the building value at risk. pending confirmation of the value at risk, our comments on adequacy of sum insured and application of average are reserved. report wording bank clause sighted the policy / certificate is noted to be subject to full value clause. adequacy reviewed we have reviewed the adequacy of the sum insured against the assessed value at risk. sum insured adequate the sum insured appears adequate against the assessed value at risk. accordingly, no average is applied. sum insured inadequate the sum insured is inadequate against the assessed value at risk. accordingly, average is applied to the adjusted loss. adequacy pending we are unable to comment on adequacy of sum insured at this stage pending receipt of [valuation / stock records / asset listing / inventory records]. average reserved our comments on average are reserved pending confirmation of the full value at risk. other clause overrides although full value clause is noted, average has not been applied in view of [appraisement clause / first loss without average clause / agreed value provision], subject to policy wording. policy limit the adjusted loss exceeds the sum insured / policy limit. our recommendation is therefore restricted to the sum insured / policy limit, subject to excess. protective wording our comments on full value clause and adequacy of sum insured are subject to the policy schedule, basis of settlement, applicable clauses, value at risk assessment and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. common mistakes mistake 1 thinking full value clause means full claim must be paid. correct full value clause means sum insured should represent full value at risk. it does not mean automatic full settlement. mistake 2 not checking value at risk. correct under full value clause, adequacy must be reviewed unless another clause waives it. mistake 3 applying full value when first loss without average applies. correct if a section is on first loss without average basis, average is usually not applied to that section. mistake 4 ignoring policy limit. correct even if no average applies, claim cannot exceed sum insured / policy limit. key point full value clause = insure full value at risk. if sum insured is short, average may apply. it is not first loss. it is not agreed value. it is not appraisement clause. always check: sum insured value at risk basis of settlement average condition any clause waiving average policy limit excess
