documents.searchText: ia remarks — master notes ia remarks.md writing banks ia remarks — master notes purpose of ia remarks general ia remark style common ia remarks by situation 1. rough reserve / estimated loss suggested wording short version 2. loss within deductible suggested wording 3. pending inventory / segregation suggested wording 4. pending technical / repairer verification suggested wording short version 5. specialist / contractor appointment required suggested wording 6. certificate / policy schedule not provided suggested wording 7. location of loss different from policy location suggested wording short version 8. no relevant endorsement suggested wording short version 9. wear and tear / gradual deterioration suggested wording short version 10. poor construction / defective workmanship suggested wording short version 11. no insured peril identified suggested wording 12. late notification / breach of loss notification clause suggested wording short version 13. repairs already carried out before inspection suggested wording 14. claim requires another policy / section suggested wording 15. public liability angle suggested wording 16. risk improvement suggested wording 17. theft / missing items suggested wording 18. car / engineering claim issues points to check suggested wording 19. iar / machinery / technical claim issues points to check suggested wording ia remarks — quick copy templates pending documents liability reserved provisional reserve rough estimate awaiting technical report no cover apparent different risk location no endorsement wear and tear repair before inspection insurer approval required key reminder knowledge notebooklm ia remarks — master notes purpose of ia remarks ia remarks are used to briefly advise insurers / operators on the current position of the claim. the remarks usually explain: what happened what is known so far what is still pending whether liability appears doubtful whether policy / certificate issue exists whether reserve is only provisional whether further documents, testing, inspection, or approval is required general ia remark style keep remarks short and direct. state only what is known at ia stage. use words like: initial enquiries revealed it would appear we are presently awaiting the loss is currently estimated subject to policy terms and conditions kindly advise please provide we seek insurer’s / operator’s approval avoid confirming liability too early. avoid using final conclusion unless evidence is clear. if uncertain, state that the matter is pending further verification. 2. loss within deductible use when estimated loss appears below deductible. still mention that estimate is rough if assessment is not final. suggested wording we wish to advise that the loss is currently estimated at rm[amount], which appears to be within the applicable deductible of rm[amount]. the above is a rough estimation based on our preliminary inspection. we will update insurers should the quantum change after further verification. 3. pending inventory / segregation use especially for stock, flood, fire, water damage, or large commercial losses. suggested wording the full extent of damage to the stock can only be determined after comprehensive segregation and inventory are carried out. we have requested the insured / participant to prepare a detailed list of damaged and salvageable items. our assessment will be reviewed upon receipt of the supporting documents. 4. pending technical / repairer verification use for machinery, equipment, electrical items, lightning, water damage, or breakdown claims. suggested wording we are presently awaiting the conclusion of the testing / verification exercise by the insured’s appointed supplier / repairer. the cause and extent of damage can only be confirmed upon receipt of the technical report. in the meantime, our reserve remains provisional. short version liability and quantum are pending technical verification. 5. specialist / contractor appointment required use when damage is extensive or repair scope cannot be confirmed by adjuster alone. common for building, structural, engineering, fire, storm, flood, or water damage claims. suggested wording in view of the extensive damage sustained, we suggest that an independent contractor / specialist be engaged to assist in determining the required scope of repair works. we seek insurer’s / operator’s approval to appoint [contractor / specialist] to inspect the damage and prepare a quotation for the necessary repair works. please let us have your further instructions. 6. certificate / policy schedule not provided use when coverage cannot be reviewed because policy / certificate schedule is missing. suggested wording kindly provide us with a copy of the policy / certificate schedule for our review. pending receipt of the policy / certificate schedule, we are unable to comment on policy liability. our advice on liability is therefore reserved at this stage. 7. location of loss different from policy location use when the situation of loss does not match the risk address in the policy / certificate. suggested wording please note that the location of risk appears to be different from the situation of loss. kindly advise whether there is another policy / certificate covering the actual situation of loss. pending confirmation, policy / certificate liability is reserved. short version the risk location differs from the situation of loss. please advise whether separate cover applies. 8. no relevant endorsement use when the policy / certificate does not carry the required extension. examples: no bursting / overflowing of water tanks apparatus or pipes endorsement no storm and tempest extension no flood extension no machinery breakdown endorsement no theft / burglary extension suggested wording our review of the policy / certificate showed that it is not endorsed with [name of endorsement]. in the absence of the relevant endorsement, the claim may fall outside the scope of cover. kindly confirm if there is any other policy / certificate or endorsement applicable to this loss. short version the relevant extension does not appear to be endorsed. liability is therefore doubtful, subject to insurer’s / operator’s confirmation. 9. wear and tear / gradual deterioration use when damage appears due to age, deterioration, poor condition, or ordinary wear and tear. common for roof cracks, floor tiles, ceiling, pipes, machinery, and building defects. suggested wording initial enquiries revealed that the damage could be due to wear and tear / gradual deterioration. such damage may not be covered under the policy / certificate. we have explained the matter to the insured / participant / agent and they are aware of the position. short version the damage appears to be due to wear and tear and may not be covered. 10. poor construction / defective workmanship use when damage appears due to poor construction, defective works, faulty installation, or workmanship issue. suggested wording initial enquiries revealed that the damage could be due to poor construction and/or defective workmanship. at this stage, we could not identify any insured peril that caused the damage. policy / certificate liability is therefore doubtful, subject to further evidence. short version the damage appears to arise from poor construction / defective workmanship rather than an insured peril. 11. no insured peril identified use when the cause does not match any covered peril. suggested wording at this stage, we are unable to trace any insured peril that would respond to the damage in question. the cause appears to be [wear and tear / poor construction / gradual deterioration / unknown], subject to further verification. we will update insurers / operators should further evidence become available. 12. late notification / breach of loss notification clause use when claim is reported late. important where policy has 30 day notification clause. also mention prejudice if repairs were carried out before inspection. suggested wording the loss occurred on [date], but we were only assigned / notified on [date]. the policy / certificate is subject to the loss notification clause of [number] days. it would appear that the insured / participant is in breach of the loss notification clause. during our survey, we noted that repair works had already been carried out. insurer’s / operator’s rights may have been prejudiced as we were not given the opportunity to verify the damage before repairs were effected. short version there was late notification and repair works had already been carried out before our inspection. insurer’s / operator’s rights may have been prejudiced. 13. repairs already carried out before inspection use when damage was repaired before adjuster could inspect. suggested wording at the time of our inspection, repair works had already been carried out. we were therefore unable to verify the original nature and extent of damage. insurer’s / operator’s position may be prejudiced. we have requested supporting documents and photographs taken before repair. 14. claim requires another policy / section use when damage may fall under another policy or section. examples: fire / all risks instead of machinery breakdown public liability instead of fire contractor’s all risks instead of property machinery breakdown endorsement required suggested wording the present policy / certificate may not respond to the cause of loss. kindly forward the relevant [fire / all risks / public liability / machinery breakdown / car / ear] policy / certificate, if any, for our review. pending receipt of the relevant policy / certificate, our comments on liability are reserved. 15. public liability angle use when damage affects third party property. common for water leakage, contractor works, tenant / landlord issues. suggested wording we note that the incident may involve damage to third party property. kindly confirm whether a public liability policy / certificate is available. we will review liability under the relevant section once the policy / certificate and supporting documents are received. 16. risk improvement use when risk condition should be improved after loss. common for theft, burglary, repeated water damage, fire, lightning, security issues. suggested wording we recommend the following risk improvement measures: improve lighting at the front and rear portion of the premises. install clocking points around the risk premises to ensure guards carry out patrol duties diligently. install / improve cctv coverage. improve physical security. review maintenance procedure. install surge protection / lightning protection, if relevant. repair defective drainage / roofing / piping, if relevant. 17. theft / missing items use when loss involves missing property or suspected theft. suggested wording initial enquiries revealed that the loss involved [description of item]. we have requested the insured / participant to provide ownership documents, purchase invoices, police report, photographs, and supporting records. we also recommend that risk improvement measures be considered to reduce recurrence. 18. car / engineering claim issues use for contractor all risks / engineering losses. points to check date of loss vs date of appointment. loss notification clause. whether repair was done before inspection. whether damaged works are part of contract works. whether defect, design, material, or workmanship exclusion applies. whether design risk extension applies. whether the extension only deletes design exclusion but still excludes defective material / workmanship. whether resultant damage is separable from defective part. suggested wording the loss occurred on [date], but we were only appointed on [date]. the certificate is subject to the loss notification clause. it would appear that the participant may be in breach of the notification condition. we also noted that repair works had been carried out before our inspection. operator’s rights may have been prejudiced as we were unable to verify the original damage. we note that the certificate is extended to include designer’s risk; however, defective material and/or workmanship may still be excluded. liability is therefore reserved pending further review. 19. iar / machinery / technical claim issues use when machinery or industrial equipment is involved. points to check was the damage sudden and accidental? was there mechanical or electrical breakdown? was the damaged item covered under iar or machinery breakdown endorsement? is the cause excluded under the main policy? is testing / verification pending? is full extent of damage known? is deductible higher than estimated loss? suggested wording the damage appears to involve machinery / equipment and the cause remains pending technical verification. we are awaiting the supplier’s / repairer’s report to confirm the cause and extent of damage. the reserve is provisional at this stage. kindly confirm whether the policy is endorsed with machinery breakdown / loss of profit extension, if applicable. ia remarks — quick copy templates pending documents kindly provide us with the following documents for our further review: policy / certificate schedule completed claim form photographs of damage repair quotation purchase invoice / ownership document technical report police report, if applicable maintenance record, if applicable liability reserved pending receipt of the above documents and completion of our enquiries, our comments on policy / certificate liability are reserved. provisional reserve the reserve recommended is provisional at this stage and may be revised once the full extent of damage is ascertained. rough estimate the amount stated is only a rough estimation based on our preliminary inspection. awaiting technical report we are presently awaiting the repairer’s / supplier’s / specialist’s report to confirm the cause and extent of damage. no cover apparent based on our initial review, the cause of damage does not appear to fall within the scope of cover, subject to insurer’s / operator’s confirmation. different risk location the situation of loss differs from the risk location stated in the policy / certificate. kindly advise whether another policy / certificate applies. no endorsement the policy / certificate does not appear to be endorsed with the relevant extension. kindly confirm whether any separate endorsement or policy applies. wear and tear initial enquiries revealed that the damage could be due to wear and tear / gradual deterioration and may not be covered. repair before inspection repair works had been carried out before our inspection. we were therefore unable to verify the original nature and extent of damage. insurer approval required we seek insurer’s / operator’s approval to appoint an independent contractor / specialist to inspect the damage and prepare a quotation. key reminder ia remarks should not read like a final report. ia is for early advice. use cautious wording. state what is known. state what is pending. highlight policy / certificate issues early. keep liability reserved if documents, inspection, or technical findings are incomplete.

liability admitted, declined, or pending? iar / car / ear / machinery breakdown / engineering iar / car / ear / engineering claim — investigation note structure circumstances what happened? describe the incident in simple terms. example: machinery broke down, contract works collapsed, equipment was damaged, electrical panel failed, crane/plant overturned, or water/fire/impact damaged insured property. when did it happen? date and time of loss. date and time damage was discovered. whether loss happened during operation, construction, erection, testing, commissioning, maintenance, or shutdown. where did it happen? risk location / project site / factory / plant room / construction area. specific location of damaged property. example: production line, switch room, basement, roof level, project zone, machinery room, substation, or temporary works area. who was involved? insured / participant. principal / employer. main contractor. subcontractor. operator. maintenance contractor. oem / supplier. consultant engineer. third party, if any. what property was damaged? building / structure. contract works. erection works. plant and machinery. electrical equipment. testing equipment. temporary works. contractor’s plant and equipment. principal’s existing property. third party property. what was the activity at the time of loss? normal operation. construction work. installation / erection. testing and commissioning. maintenance / servicing. repair works. loading / unloading. lifting operation. trial run. shutdown / start up. what immediate action was taken? stopped work / stopped machine. isolated power supply. shut down affected system. secured the area. notified site management / insurer / broker. called contractor / repairer / oem. preserved damaged parts. carried out temporary repair. prevented further damage. what is the current status? damage still unrepaired. temporary repair completed. permanent repair completed. machine still down. project works delayed. production affected. further expert inspection required. cause what is the alleged cause? mechanical breakdown. electrical breakdown. short circuit. overheating. overloading. operator error. impact / collision. collapse. fire / explosion. water damage. defective workmanship. defective design. defective material. lack of maintenance. wear and tear. testing / commissioning failure. external accidental damage. what is the proximate cause? identify the dominant cause of the damage. do not rely only on insured’s version. confirm through inspection, documents, technical report, and physical evidence. is the damage sudden and accidental? yes / no / pending confirmation. engineering claims usually require clear evidence of sudden and unforeseen physical damage. if damage developed gradually, check exclusions carefully. is there a defective part? identify the failed component. example: bearing, motor, gearbox, cable, panel, control card, pipe, weld, structural member, foundation, or design element. separate the defective part from the resultant damage. is there resultant damage? identify damage caused by the initial failure. example: failed bearing damaged shaft and housing; defective weld caused collapse; short circuit damaged control panel. check whether policy covers resultant damage but excludes the defective part itself. was the damage due to wear and tear? check age of item. check maintenance record. check service history. check previous complaints. check corrosion, fatigue, rust, deterioration, or repeated failure. was the damage due to maintenance issue? poor maintenance. lack of servicing. failure to follow manufacturer recommendation. no maintenance record. delayed repair. continued operation despite warning signs. was any contractor / supplier / repairer responsible? check if damage arose from defective workmanship, faulty installation, or repair error. check warranty. check contract terms. check whether recovery is possible. machinery breakdown wording in your source refers to exclusion for loss or damage for which a supplier, contractor, or repairer is responsible by law or contract. :contentreference[oaicite:0]{index=0} is specialist opinion required? mechanical engineer. electrical engineer. civil / structural engineer. forensic engineer. oem / manufacturer. quantity surveyor. testing laboratory. what evidence supports the cause? photos. damaged parts. maintenance log. service report. oem report. contractor report. incident report. cctv. testing records. alarm history. scada / control panel data. site diary. method statement. policy / certificate liability what policy applies? iar — industrial all risks. car — contractors’ all risks. ear — erection all risks. mb — machinery breakdown. cpm — contractors’ plant and machinery. engineering policy / certificate. bi / lop extension, if applicable. is the date of loss within the policy period? confirm policy inception and expiry. for car / ear, confirm whether loss occurred during construction, erection, testing, commissioning, maintenance, or defects liability period. is the damaged property insured? check schedule. check item description. check location. check sum insured / sum covered. check whether the damaged item is listed or falls within insured property. which section responds? material damage. contract works. erection works. machinery breakdown. contractor’s plant and machinery. principal’s existing property. third party liability. business interruption / loss of profit. for iar, what to check? is there physical loss or damage? is the property insured? is the cause accidental and not excluded? is machinery breakdown excluded under the main section? is there a specific machinery breakdown endorsement? one internal report noted mechanical/electrical breakdown exclusion under one section, but also noted a specific machinery breakdown & loss of profit endorsement relevant to liability. :contentreference[oaicite:1]{index=1} for car, what to check? is the damaged property part of contract works? has the work been handed over? is the loss before completion / taking over? is the damaged item temporary works or permanent works? is principal’s existing property involved? is defective workmanship / design exclusion relevant? is only resultant damage payable? for ear, what to check? is the damaged property part of erection works? did the loss occur during installation, testing, or commissioning? was testing period covered? was the damaged item already taken over? was the failure due to design, material, workmanship, or operational error? is resultant damage separable from defective part? for machinery breakdown, what to check? is the machine listed in the schedule? was it in operation or at rest? was the breakdown sudden and unforeseen? was it due to mechanical or electrical breakdown? was there any existing defect before policy commencement? was damage due to supplier / contractor / repairer responsibility? is the sum covered adequate? your source states the sum covered should equal the replacement cost of new machinery of the same kind and capacity, including freight, duties, customs duties, and erection cost. :contentreference[oaicite:2]{index=2} for cpm, what to check? is the plant / equipment listed? was it within insured location / territorial limit? was it operating, parked, transported, lifted, or dismantled? was damage caused by overturning, impact, collision, fire, theft, or breakdown? is operator negligence involved? is maintenance / wear and tear exclusion relevant? for third party liability, what to check? was third party property damaged? was there bodily injury? is the insured legally liable? did liability arise from construction / erection / operation? is contractual liability excluded? is subcontractor or contractor responsible? is recovery possible? what exclusions must be checked? wear and tear. gradual deterioration. corrosion / rust. lack of maintenance. existing fault or defect. defective design. defective material. defective workmanship. manufacturer / supplier / contractor responsibility. mechanical or electrical breakdown, unless endorsed. testing and commissioning limitation. consequential loss, unless bi / lop is covered. inventory shortage. unexplained disappearance. is bi / lop involved? is there insured material damage first? did the material damage cause business interruption? what is the interruption period? what is the indemnity period? what production / sales loss is claimed? are accounts and production records available? is mb lop / bi extension endorsed? what is the liability position? liability admitted. liability declined. liability reserved pending documents. liability subject to specialist report. liability subject to policy interpretation. liability subject to separation between defective part and resultant damage. quantum / adjustment what is being claimed? repair cost. replacement cost. labour. spare parts. oem charges. testing and commissioning. freight. customs duty. erection cost. temporary repair. debris removal. professional fees. bi / lop loss. is repair possible? repairable. beyond economical repair. replacement required. oem recommendation required. temporary repair acceptable. is there betterment? new part replacing old part. upgrade in capacity. improved specification. modernisation. design improvement. deduct betterment if applicable. is there depreciation? age of machine / property. condition before loss. usage. maintenance record. settlement basis: indemnity or reinstatement. any deduction? excess / deductible. salvage. betterment. depreciation. average / underinsurance. non payable defective part. non policy items. suggested final wording what happened? the insured reported damage to [item/property] at [location] on [date], during [operation/construction/testing/maintenance]. cause based on our inspection and available documents, the damage appears to have been caused by [cause]. the incident appears to be sudden and accidental / mechanical breakdown / electrical breakdown / defect related / gradual in nature. the defective component is identified as [component]. the resultant damage affects [items]. policy / certificate liability the claim is considered under [iar/car/ear/mb/cpm] policy. the date of loss falls within the policy period. the damaged property is insured under [section/item]. liability is subject to policy terms, conditions, exclusions, excess, and limits. the defective part / excluded cause should be separated from resultant damage. based on available evidence, we recommend liability be [admitted/declined/reserved pending further documents].

documents.title: Lightning - Circumstances
documents.excerpt: When writing the "Circumstances" or "Discovery" section for a lightning claim, adjusters rely on a very consistent, predictable formula to tell the story of what happened. Think of it like describing the environment before a sleep study—you want to establish exactly what the cond
documents.searchText: lightning circumstances lightning circumstances.md peril playbooks when writing the "circumstances" or "discovery" section for a lightning claim, adjusters rely on a very consistent, predictable formula to tell the story of what happened. think of it like describing the environment before a sleep study—you want to establish exactly what the conditions were and what immediately triggered the disruption. based on the sources, here are the standard details you would normally comment on, along with the exact phrasing used in the industry: 1. establish the weather conditions you always start by confirming that a storm actually happened at the specific time of the incident, usually based on an eyewitness account. the standard, boilerplate phrasing used across almost all reports is: "we were informed by [name and title of the person] that a heavy downpour accompanied by thunder and lightning [or 'followed by lightning strikes'] was experienced at the material time". 2. describe the immediate disruption next, you describe the sudden electrical failure or event that tipped the insured off to the problem. you will usually link the lightning directly to a power trip. practical examples from the reports include: "following a loud thunderclap, the premises suddenly experienced a power trip". "following a series of lightning strikes, [the person] received a notification from the security guard that the [equipment] had malfunctioned". 3. explain the discovery of the damage then, you explain exactly how the insured realized specific items were broken. this usually happens right after they try to turn the electricity back on. a standard comment looks like this: "upon restoring the power supply, she noticed that the cctv system was not functioning". "further checks revealed that the auto gate was faulty and could not be opened as well". 4. state the immediate action taken finally, you wrap up the circumstances by stating what the insured did right after finding the damage to get things fixed. the go to phrasing here is simple and direct: "a repairer was contacted to rectify the problem". 5. weather verification (optional but common) while sometimes placed in the "cause" section, adjusters frequently back up these circumstances by confirming the weather data. you would normally comment: "we have also checked into the weather condition on the material day and noted that a thunderstorm was indeed recorded at the reported time" lightning lightning

documents.title: Lightning - technical report
documents.excerpt: Think of a technical report for a lightning claim like a diagnostic report from a sleep clinic. It tells us exactly what went wrong with the equipment, why it stopped working, and how to fix it
documents.searchText: lightning technical report lightning technical report.md peril playbooks think of a technical report for a lightning claim like a diagnostic report from a sleep clinic. it tells us exactly what went wrong with the equipment, why it stopped working, and how to fix it . when a piece of equipment breaks after a storm, the repair technician inspects it and writes up this report . based on the sources, here is what that report typically includes: 1. the diagnosis (cause of damage) just like a sleep doctor diagnoses sleep apnea, the technician officially confirms the root cause of the breakdown. the report will typically state that the item was damaged by a "power surge" or a "strong voltage surge" caused by a lightning strike . this is crucial because it proves to the insurance company that lightning was actually the culprit . 2. the symptoms (what exactly broke) the report gets specific about which internal parts failed. technicians will often note that the "sensitive electronic components," such as circuit boards or power cards, were ruined by the surge . they will also document physical evidence, frequently mentioning that they found "burnt marks" or short circuits on the power input areas or main fuses . 3. the testing results the technician doesn't just guess; the report will mention that they actively ran tests or "troubleshooting" on the affected components to confirm they were completely faulty and beyond simple repair . 4. the prescription (scope of repairs and cost) finally, the technical report is usually combined with a repair quotation or invoice . it will provide a detailed list of the "scope of repairs," outlining the specific replacement parts needed (like new motherboards, capacitors, or wiring), the labor charges, and the total cost required to bring the machine back to life. lightning.

documents.title: Lightning Cause
documents.excerpt: Damage in lightning claims is overwhelmingly attributed to a strong voltage or power surge induced by a lightning strike. This destructive surge can occur from either a direct lightning strike to a building's roof or structure, or from a "proximity strike" hitting somewhere in th

headings.text: Lightning Cause
Meaning of Lightning
Causes of Lightning
How Lightning Forms
Terms Related to Lightning
Thunder
Electric Discharge
Suggested Sentences for Understanding
Summary

documents.searchText: lightning cause lightning cause.md peril playbooks lightning cause meaning of lightning causes of lightning how lightning forms terms related to lightning thunder electric discharge suggested sentences for understanding summary damage in lightning claims is overwhelmingly attributed to a strong voltage or power surge induced by a lightning strike. this destructive surge can occur from either a direct lightning strike to a building's roof or structure, or from a "proximity strike" hitting somewhere in the nearby vicinity. typically, the event begins during a heavy downpour when a thunderclap or series of lightning strikes triggers a sudden electrical pulse, causing the premises' main electrical distribution board or elcb to trip and cut off the power supply. when the power is eventually restored, occupants usually discover that the massive wave of excess electricity has overwhelmed and ruined the sensitive electronic circuitry of their equipment. the items most vulnerable and frequently destroyed by these surges are those reliant on delicate electronic boards, such as cctv systems, passenger lift circuit boards (pcbs), auto gate controllers, weighbridge indicators, and household appliances like televisions and air conditioners. to officially confirm that a lightning induced surge was the root cause, adjusters and repair technicians conduct diagnostic testing and look for physical evidence. they frequently identify the cause by finding short circuits and distinct "burnt marks" on the affected electronic components, particularly around the power input areas or main fuses. lightning can you make all the above in keypoints? with readable heading etc i.e. meanings, terms etc. suggested sentences also from my sources. lightning cause meaning of lightning lightning is a natural electrical discharge that occurs during thunderstorms. it appears as a bright flash of light in the sky. causes of lightning lightning is caused by the buildup of electrical charges within clouds and between clouds and the earth's surface. when there is a difference in electrical charge, it results in a sudden discharge known as lightning. how lightning forms during a storm, wind causes ice particles within clouds to collide, leading to separation of charges. positive charges gather at the top of the cloud, while negative charges accumulate at the bottom. the negative charge at the cloud's base induces a positive charge on the earth's surface below. when the electric potential becomes high enough, it results in a discharge or lightning bolt. terms related to lightning thunder the sound produced by the rapid expansion of air due to lightning's heat. electric discharge the flow of electricity between regions of differing electric potential, seen as lightning. suggested sentences for understanding "lightning occurs because of electrical charges building up in clouds." "when these charges become too strong, they are released as lightning." "thunder is caused by the rapid heating and expansion of air around lightning." summary lightning is caused by electrical charge differences created during thunderstorms, leading to a sudden discharge that we see as bright flashes in the sky.

documents.title: Lightning Claim — Adjuster Investigation / Review Scope
documents.excerpt: Lightning Claim — Adjuster Investigation / Review Scope

headings.text: Lightning Claim — Adjuster Investigation / Review Scope

documents.searchText: lightning claim — adjuster investigation / review scope lightning.md peril playbooks lightning claim — adjuster investigation / review scope investigation lightning claim — adjuster investigation / review scope confirm basic claim details date and time of loss location / risk premises insured’s description of incident item(s) allegedly damaged amount claimed policy type and coverage applicable establish alleged cause of loss direct lightning strike proximity lightning strike power surge due to lightning electrical surge / fluctuation not necessarily caused by lightning interview insured / representative ask what happened before, during, and after the incident confirm whether there was heavy rain, thunder, lightning, power trip, blackout, or electrical surge record the insured’s timeline clearly inspect damaged item(s) identify damaged equipment such as cctv, recorder, tv, home automation system, electrical panel, or electronic components check whether damage pattern is consistent with lightning or surge confirm whether item is repairable or beyond economical repair obtain technical / repairer report ask repairer to confirm cause of damage ask whether damage is consistent with lightning, surge, or internal defect ask whether item can be repaired obtain repair quotation or replacement quotation treat “suspected lightning / power surge” as inconclusive unless supported by other evidence check lightning data obtain tnb research / lightning data report check date of loss and nearby dates check whether lightning activity was recorded near the risk location if no lightning activity is recorded, highlight inconsistency with the insured’s version compare insured version with objective evidence compare insured’s statement compare repairer’s opinion compare tnb lightning report compare weather condition compare damage pattern compare timing of failure determine proximate cause lightning electrical surge power fluctuation wear and tear / internal defect unknown electrical failure avoid confirming lightning unless supported by evidence review policy response check whether policy covers lightning check whether policy covers electrical surge or power surge check whether policy is all risks, fire, or machinery breakdown check exclusions for electrical / mechanical breakdown check exclusions for wear and tear check exclusions for defective parts or unexplained failure check applicable excess / deductible validate ownership and insurable interest obtain purchase invoice obtain asset listing obtain inventory list obtain leasing agreement, if applicable confirm item belongs to insured or is under insured’s responsibility assess quantum review repair cost review replacement cost verify market price apply depreciation or betterment if required deduct salvage, excess, or deductible confirm settlement basis: indemnity or reinstatement consider risk improvement recommendation install surge protection system review lightning arrestor system review main electrical distribution board install lightning arrestor, if required remind insurer/insured that mitigation cannot fully rule out recurrence prepare report to insurer circumstances of loss cause investigation tnb lightning data result repairer’s findings policy coverage view quantum assessment recommendation on liability risk improvement recommendation key point do not accept lightning claim only because insured or repairer says “lightning” or “power surge” verify with tnb lightning data, physical evidence, technical evidence, and policy wording so, in simple terms: the lightning creates a massive wave of excess electricity, that wave travels through the building's wires, and it overloads the machine's most delicate electronic parts until they literally burn out.

documents.searchText: machinery breakdown (mb) claim — investigation aspect machinery breakdown.md peril playbooks machinery breakdown (mb) claim — investigation aspect simple meaning key point circumstances what happened? when did it happen? suggested wording machine verification what to confirm? documents to request suggested wording cause investigation main questions documents to request suggested wording policy / certificate liability what to check? common issues suggested wording maintenance review what to check? documents to request suggested wording quantum / adjustment what to check? documents to request suggested wording application samples 1. motor winding burnt investigation suggested wording 2. bearing failure due to wear and tear investigation suggested wording 3. gearbox damaged due to lack of lubrication investigation suggested wording 4. breakdown during repair / maintenance investigation suggested wording 5. damage caused by lightning / power surge investigation suggested wording 6. machine beyond economical repair investigation suggested wording 7. obsolete spare part investigation suggested wording 8. machine not listed in policy investigation suggested wording 9. loss of profit / downtime claim investigation suggested wording report wording bank machine verification cause pending maintenance pending sudden breakdown wear and tear / maintenance issue quantum liability reserved key reminder investigation machinery breakdown (mb) claim — investigation aspect simple meaning mb claim involves sudden and unforeseen mechanical or electrical breakdown of insured machinery. investigation must confirm: machine involved failed component cause of failure whether failure was sudden and accidental whether failure was due to wear and tear, poor maintenance or pre existing defect whether machine is insured under the mb policy whether repair cost is reasonable key point do not accept “machine rosak” only. need technical evidence from repairer, specialist or manufacturer. machine verification what to confirm? is the machine listed in the policy schedule? is the machine at the insured location? is the machine owned / leased by the insured / participant? was it operating before breakdown? is it under warranty or maintenance contract? documents to request mb policy / certificate schedule machine listing / schedule purchase invoice asset register lease / hire purchase agreement, if applicable machine nameplate photo make, model and serial number year of manufacture installation / commissioning date location of machine suggested wording we have requested the policy schedule, asset register, purchase invoice and machine nameplate details to verify whether the affected machine is insured under the mb policy. cause investigation main questions what is the proximate cause of breakdown? was it sudden and unforeseen? was it mechanical or electrical failure? was it due to: wear and tear lack of maintenance overload operator error pre existing defect gradual deterioration corrosion / rust / scaling power surge / voltage fluctuation improper installation supplier / repairer responsibility documents to request technician / specialist report breakdown report failure analysis / diagnostic report error code report operator logbook maintenance / service records repair history photos of damaged component electrical test / insulation resistance test, if relevant oil analysis / vibration analysis, if relevant suggested wording the proximate cause of breakdown remains subject to technical confirmation. we have requested technical report, maintenance records, operator log and photographs of damaged components to determine whether the failure was sudden and accidental or due to excluded causes. policy / certificate liability what to check? policy / certificate period risk location description of insured machine sum insured / sum covered deductible / excess basis of settlement maintenance warranty / condition exclusions whether machine is listed whether breakdown occurred during policy period whether cause falls within mb cover common issues machine not listed machine at different location wear and tear lack of maintenance pre existing defect supplier / repairer responsibility fire / lightning / flood / water damage cause repair cost includes betterment replacement of whole machine when only part failed suggested wording policy liability is subject to confirmation that the affected machine is insured under the mb policy and that the proximate cause falls within the scope of cover. our comments are subject to the policy terms, exclusions, conditions, warranties, sum insured and deductible. maintenance review what to check? was the machine regularly serviced? any overdue maintenance? any prior abnormal noise, vibration or overheating? any previous breakdown? any temporary repair before loss? any technician recommendation not complied with? any operation outside normal range? documents to request preventive maintenance schedule maintenance contract service invoices operator daily checklist running hours record lubrication records calibration records alarm / temperature / pressure logs previous technician recommendations suggested wording we have requested maintenance and service records to determine whether the machine was properly maintained prior to breakdown. if the failure is due to lack of maintenance, gradual deterioration or pre existing defect, policy liability may be affected. quantum / adjustment what to check? is repair necessary? is replacement necessary? is quotation reasonable? is only the damaged part replaced? any betterment / upgrading? any obsolete part issue? any salvage value? is labour cost reasonable? is dismantling / reinstallation cost included? is testing after repair included? is loss of production claimed? documents to request repair quotation replacement quotation parts and labour breakdown tax invoice / receipt, if repaired manufacturer recommendation confirmation whether repairable salvage value / disposal record photos after repair testing report after repair downtime records, if lop / bi involved suggested wording our adjustment is based on the reasonable cost to repair / replace the damaged component arising from the insured breakdown. any betterment, upgrading, unrelated maintenance or pre existing defect rectification should be excluded or separately considered. application samples 1. motor winding burnt investigation confirm motor forms part of insured machine. request technician report. request electrical / insulation test. check maintenance record. check whether due to overload, voltage issue or wear and tear. suggested wording the claim concerns breakdown of the motor forming part of the insured machine. based on preliminary findings, the motor winding was found burnt. we have requested electrical test report, maintenance records and repair quotation to determine the proximate cause and reasonable repair cost. policy liability is subject to confirmation that the failure was sudden and unforeseen and not due to excluded causes. 2. bearing failure due to wear and tear investigation check prior symptoms. check maintenance and lubrication records. check whether failure was gradual. check whether bearing is a consumable / wear item. suggested wording the available information indicates that the bearing had shown signs of abnormal noise prior to failure. the technician’s findings suggest gradual wear rather than sudden accidental breakdown. policy liability appears doubtful, subject to insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. 3. gearbox damaged due to lack of lubrication investigation check lubrication record. check operator checklist. check service history. request technician report. suggested wording the gearbox failure appears to be associated with insufficient lubrication. we have requested maintenance and lubrication records to determine whether the machine was properly maintained. if the failure is confirmed to have arisen from lack of maintenance, policy liability may be affected. 4. breakdown during repair / maintenance investigation check whether machine was under repair at time of damage. check repairer responsibility. check service contract. consider recovery. suggested wording the breakdown reportedly occurred during repair / maintenance works carried out by [repairer]. we have requested service report, repairer’s explanation and maintenance agreement to determine responsibility. recovery against the repairer should be considered if damage arose from their negligence or workmanship. 5. damage caused by lightning / power surge investigation check whether cause is mb breakdown or external peril. request technician report. request tnb / lightning report, if relevant. check if fire / iar / ee / all risks policy is more suitable. suggested wording the cause of damage is reported as possible lightning / power surge. we recommend insurers / operators review whether the loss falls under the mb policy or should be considered under fire / iar / ee / all risks cover. technical report and lightning / power supply evidence are required for further review. 6. machine beyond economical repair investigation request written confirmation beyond repair. request repair estimate and replacement quotation. check age, condition and basis of settlement. check salvage value. suggested wording the repairer has advised that the machine is beyond economical repair. we have requested repair estimate, replacement quotation, machine age, condition details and salvage value to assess the loss in accordance with the policy basis of settlement. 7. obsolete spare part investigation confirm part obsolescence. check alternative repair. check whether full replacement is necessary. avoid betterment. suggested wording the damaged component is reportedly obsolete and no longer available. we have requested manufacturer confirmation and alternative repair / replacement options. the adjustment should be limited to the reasonable cost of reinstating the damaged component / machine to its pre loss condition, without betterment. 8. machine not listed in policy investigation check policy schedule. check endorsements. check asset list submitted at underwriting. ask insurers / operators to confirm coverage. suggested wording we were unable to identify the affected machine in the policy schedule / list of insured machinery. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the machine is insured under the mb policy. pending confirmation, policy liability is reserved. 9. loss of profit / downtime claim investigation check if mb loss of profit cover exists. request production records. request sales records. confirm repair timeline and actual downtime. check mitigation / alternative production. suggested wording the insured has indicated possible loss of production arising from the machinery breakdown. we recommend confirmation whether machinery breakdown loss of profit cover is in force. any consequential loss claim will require production, sales, financial and downtime records. report wording bank machine verification we have requested the machine schedule, asset register and nameplate details to confirm whether the affected machine is insured under the policy. cause pending the proximate cause of breakdown remains subject to technical confirmation by the repairer / specialist / manufacturer. maintenance pending maintenance and service records have been requested to determine whether the machine was properly maintained prior to breakdown. sudden breakdown based on available information, the breakdown appears sudden and unforeseen, subject to technical confirmation and policy terms. wear and tear / maintenance issue the available evidence suggests possible wear and tear / gradual deterioration / lack of maintenance. policy liability may be affected, subject to the full policy wording and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. quantum our adjustment is based on reasonable cost to repair / replace the damaged component directly arising from the breakdown. betterment, improvement, unrelated maintenance or pre existing defect rectification should be excluded. liability reserved policy liability is reserved pending confirmation of the affected machine, cause of breakdown, maintenance history, applicable exclusions, sum insured and deductible. key reminder for mb claim, always ask: is the machine insured? what part failed? why did it fail? was it sudden and unforeseen? was maintenance properly done? is there wear and tear / gradual deterioration? is repair cost reasonable? is there betterment? is there supplier / repairer responsibility? is loss of profit being claimed separately? can you also provide proper question to ask to the insured, act like i'm asking a question to 1. insured, 2. contractor 3. third party in my profession manner. use word like justification, incurred, respectively, subsequently & relevant etc as related to my job as loss adjuster. like mock up scenario. not to use complex jargon and a bit straigh forward question. certainly! here are the tailored questions for each party, incorporating the specified words and maintaining a professional yet straightforward tone suitable for a loss adjuster: questions to the insured: 1. could you please provide justification for the recent machinery breakdown and clarify if any maintenance issues might have contributed to the incident? 2. can you specify whether any costs incurred due to repairs or replacements were subsequently documented and supported by relevant invoices? 3. please confirm if all necessary safety protocols were followed prior to the breakdown, and if you have any relevant records or reports related to the machinery’s condition beforehand. questions to the contractor: 1. could you explain the justification for your assessment of the machinery failure and whether your inspection identified any underlying issues? 2. were the costs incurred during repairs and replacements documented accordingly, and do you have relevant supporting evidence? 3. subsequently, did your team identify any factors that could have contributed to the breakdown which should be considered in this claim? questions to a third party (e.g., supplier or external service provider): 1. can you provide justification regarding the quality or suitability of parts supplied that may have been involved in this machinery failure? 2. were any costs incurred by your company relevant to warranty claims or service visits subsequently supported by proper documentation? 3. respectively, could you clarify if there are any known issues with similar equipment supplied previously that might be relevant to this case? let me know if you'd like these questions tailored further or in a different context!

documents.title: Maintenance Period Clause or Defects Liability Clause
documents.relativePath: Maintenance Period Clause or Defects Liability Clause.md
documents.excerpt: Extends cover after practical completion / handover for a defined maintenance period.

headings.text: Crucial Application
What to check
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: maintenance period clause or defects liability clause maintenance period clause or defects liability clause.md policy & clauses crucial application what to check suggested wording endorsement clauses car contractor all risk meaning extends cover after practical completion / handover for a defined maintenance period. scope may be narrow or wide depending on wording. some maintenance cover only applies to damage caused during maintenance work. some may cover damage arising from work carried out during construction period. crucial application apply when loss is discovered after completion / handover. example: defect appears during defects liability period. contractor returns to site for rectification and causes damage. damage discovered after certificate of practical completion. what to check date of loss. date of practical completion / handover. maintenance period stated in policy. type of maintenance cover. was contractor carrying out maintenance work? did damage arise from construction work done before handover? is the damage merely defective workmanship? suggested wording the loss was discovered after practical completion / handover. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the policy was still operative under the maintenance period extension. liability is subject to the scope of maintenance cover and whether the damage falls within the maintenance clause.

documents.title: Market Value Clause
documents.excerpt: Market Value Clause means settlement is based on market value at the time of loss.

headings.text: Simple Meaning
Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: market value clause market value clause.md policy & clauses simple meaning key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses market value clause simple meaning market value clause means settlement is based on market value at the time of loss. depreciation / age / condition / wear and tear are considered. it is different from reinstatement value. key point do not pay new replacement cost if policy is market value basis. application sample facts damaged item was purchased several years ago. insured submitted replacement quotation for new item. policy is subject to market value clause. suggested wording the policy is noted to be subject to market value clause. accordingly, allowance has been applied to reflect age, condition and depreciation of the damaged item. the adjustment is based on the market value of a similar item at the time of loss, subject to policy terms, conditions and excess.

documents.title: Misdescription Clause
documents.excerpt: Misdescription Clause may protect the cover from being prejudiced by alteration / misdescription of the insured interest.

headings.text: Simple Meaning
Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: misdescription clause misdescription clause.md policy & clauses simple meaning key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses misdescription clause simple meaning misdescription clause may protect the cover from being prejudiced by alteration / misdescription of the insured interest. usually requires notice to be given to insurer / operator once the insured becomes aware. additional premium / contribution may be required from inception or date of increased risk. key point it may help minor description issues, but does not cure all material non disclosure. application sample facts policy description slightly differs from actual use / interest. insured says change was not intentional. risk may have increased. suggested wording the policy is noted to contain misdescription clause. we recommend insurers / operators consider whether the discrepancy amounts to alteration / misdescription of the insured interest and whether additional premium / contribution is required. liability remains subject to materiality of the change and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation.

documents.title: Offsite Storage Clause
documents.excerpt: Extends cover to materials stored away from the project site.

headings.text: Crucial Application
What to check
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: offsite storage clause offsite storage clause.md policy & clauses crucial application what to check suggested wording endorsement clauses car contractor all risk meaning extends cover to materials stored away from the project site. without this clause, materials outside the insured site may not be covered. crucial application apply when materials are damaged / stolen at supplier warehouse, temporary storage yard, fabrication yard or contractor’s store. example: tiles stored at offsite warehouse damaged by flood. steel materials stolen from contractor’s yard. equipment stored at supplier’s premises before delivery to site. what to check is offsite storage covered? is location declared? is there a limit? was material intended for the insured project? was material already paid for / owned by insured? was storage location within territorial limit? were security / storage warranties complied with? suggested wording the affected materials were stored away from the project site. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether offsite storage clause is endorsed and whether the storage location falls within the scope of cover. liability is reserved pending confirmation of the clause wording, location, ownership and supporting documents.

documents.searchText: outbuilding clause — claim notes outbuilding clause.md policy & clauses outbuilding clause — claim notes simple meaning easy way to remember key point outbuilding clause — adjuster review scope circumstances policy / certificate liability application samples 1. item kept inside guard house facts application suggested wording 2. item kept outside guard house / open compound facts application suggested wording 3. stock kept in open yard facts application suggested wording 4. item inside detached store room facts application suggested wording 5. open sided shed / canopy facts application suggested wording 6. container used as storage facts application suggested wording 7. pump house / generator room facts application suggested wording 8. different premises nearby facts application suggested wording report wording bank clause sighted outbuilding likely applies open compound stock kept in open grey area different location protective wording key point endorsement clauses outbuilding clause — claim notes simple meaning outbuilding clause usually extends cover to another small building / structure within the same compound of the insured premises. it is not meant to cover items kept openly anywhere in the compound. it is usually for property kept inside a separate building / enclosed structure within the compound. easy way to remember main building: the insured shop / factory / warehouse / house stated in the policy. outbuilding: a separate enclosed structure within the same compound. example: guard house store room pump house generator room detached garage external shed security post utility room open compound: outdoor area with no enclosure / no building. example: open yard car park loading bay front compound back lane outdoor storage area key point outbuilding clause may help if the item was kept inside another enclosed building within the compound. it usually does not help if the item was merely kept in the open compound. if the item was kept in the open, check whether the policy excludes stock / property kept in the open. outbuilding clause — adjuster review scope circumstances what happened? fire / burglary / theft / flood / storm / impact / water damage. where was the damaged / stolen property? inside main building? inside outbuilding? inside guard house? inside store room? inside pump room? inside detached shed? in the open compound? what is the structure? permanent building? temporary shed? guard post? container? open rack? covered but open sided structure? fully enclosed room? was the item stored inside? yes / no. if yes, identify the structure. if no, consider whether it was property kept in the open. was the outbuilding within the insured compound? same risk address? same fenced compound? same title / lot? same insured premises? adjacent premises? different location? policy / certificate liability is outbuilding clause endorsed? what does the clause say? does it cover only buildings or also contents inside? does it require the outbuilding to be within the same compound? does it apply to all perils or only certain sections? is the damaged / stolen property insured? is there any exclusion for stock / property kept in the open? is there any limit for outbuilding? is the outbuilding construction acceptable? was the outbuilding declared in the schedule? was there any change in risk / occupation? application samples 1. item kept inside guard house facts item was kept inside a guard house at the entrance of the compound. guard house is within the insured premises. guard house is enclosed and forms part of the risk compound. application this is the type of situation where outbuilding clause may apply. it is not merely property kept in the open. need to confirm the clause covers contents within outbuildings. suggested wording the affected item was kept inside the guard house located within the insured compound. the guard house appears to be a separate outbuilding within the risk premises. subject to the outbuilding clause extending to contents kept within such structure, the claim may be considered under the policy. we recommend insurers / operators confirm the applicability of the outbuilding clause and any applicable limit. 3. stock kept in open yard facts stock was kept in the open yard within the factory compound. stock was not stored inside warehouse or outbuilding. loss arose from theft / flood / storm / fire. application outbuilding clause does not usually convert open yard into covered building. if there is exclusion for stock kept in open, liability may be doubtful. suggested wording the damaged / stolen stock was kept in the open yard within the compound. the stock was not contained within the main building or any outbuilding. the outbuilding clause does not appear applicable to property kept in the open. policy liability is therefore subject to review of the exclusion for stock / property kept in the open. 4. item inside detached store room facts detached store room located behind main building. store room within same fenced compound. items stored inside and locked. loss occurred due to fire / burglary / flood. application stronger case for outbuilding clause. check whether detached store room is part of insured premises. check whether contents are covered. suggested wording the affected property was stored inside a detached store room located within the insured compound. the store room appears to fall within the meaning of an outbuilding. subject to the outbuilding clause and policy schedule, the loss may be considered under the policy. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the contents stored therein are covered and whether any sub limit applies. 5. open sided shed / canopy facts property was kept under a roof canopy. structure has roof but no walls / not fully enclosed. located within compound. application grey area. it may not be a proper outbuilding. need to review exact clause wording and insurer position. suggested wording the property was kept under an open sided shed / canopy within the compound. the structure is not fully enclosed. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether such structure falls within the definition of an outbuilding under the policy. pending confirmation, policy liability is reserved. 6. container used as storage facts container placed within compound. items kept inside container. container locked. loss occurred. application depends on wording. some insurers may not treat container as outbuilding unless specifically declared. check if container is permanent / declared / used as store. suggested wording the affected property was stored inside a container located within the insured compound. the container was used as a storage facility but does not appear to be a permanent building. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether the outbuilding clause extends to container storage. liability is reserved pending confirmation of the policy wording. 7. pump house / generator room facts pump / generator damaged inside separate pump house / generator room. structure within same compound. application usually stronger outbuilding argument. check if building and machinery are insured. suggested wording the damaged equipment was housed inside the pump house / generator room located within the insured compound. the structure appears to be an outbuilding ancillary to the insured premises. subject to the policy schedule and outbuilding clause, the loss may be considered under the relevant section. 8. different premises nearby facts item stored in separate premises nearby. not within same compound. same owner, but different lot / address. application outbuilding clause likely does not apply. need separate risk location or other policy. suggested wording the affected property was located at a separate premises and not within the insured compound. the outbuilding clause does not appear applicable as the property was not situated within an outbuilding at the insured premises. kindly advise whether there is any separate policy / certificate covering the actual location of loss. report wording bank clause sighted the policy / certificate is noted to be endorsed with outbuilding clause. we have considered whether the affected property was situated within an outbuilding at the insured premises. outbuilding likely applies the affected property was kept inside [guard house / store room / pump house / detached shed] located within the insured compound. the said structure appears to fall within the scope of an outbuilding. subject to the policy wording and any applicable sub limit, the claim may be considered under the outbuilding clause. open compound the affected property was kept in the open compound and not inside any enclosed outbuilding. the outbuilding clause does not appear applicable to property kept in the open. stock kept in open the stock was stored in the open yard within the compound. we recommend insurers / operators consider the exclusion relating to stock / property kept in the open. grey area the property was kept under an open sided structure / canopy. it is unclear whether the structure falls within the meaning of an outbuilding. we recommend insurers / operators confirm their position on the applicability of the clause. different location the property was not located within the insured compound. the outbuilding clause does not appear applicable. liability is reserved pending confirmation of the correct risk location and applicable policy. protective wording our comments are subject to the exact wording of the outbuilding clause, policy schedule, insured property description, limits, exclusions and insurers’ / operators’ final confirmation. key point outbuilding clause is about another building / enclosed structure within the compound. it is not the same as open compound cover. if item is inside guard house / store room / pump house, clause may help. if item is outside in open compound, clause may not help. always check: is the structure enclosed? is it within same compound? is it part of insured premises? are contents covered? is property kept in open excluded?and pls provide application samples as welland pls provide application samples as well

documents.title: Policy Jacket about Adequacy
documents.excerpt: To determine the adequacy of the Sum Insured, the actual total value of the insured subject matter (inclusive of Goods and Services Tax) at the time of an insured peril is compared against the policy's Sum Insured.
documents.searchText: policy jacket about adequacy policy jacket about adequacy.md policy & clauses to determine the adequacy of the sum insured, the actual total value of the insured subject matter (inclusive of goods and services tax) at the time of an insured peril is compared against the policy's sum insured. if the collective value of the property is greater than the sum insured, the coverage is deemed inadequate. in such cases, the insured is considered to be their own insurer for the difference and must bear a proportional, or rateable, share of the loss. if the policy covers multiple items, this condition is applied to each item separately. the sources also highlight how tax entitlements affect this calculation. if you are entitled to an input tax credit on the insured items, the total value used to determine the adequacy of the sum insured will be reduced by deducting your input tax credit entitlement. adequacy of sum insured.

documents.title: Principal’s Existing Property Clause or Existing Property Extension
documents.relativePath: Principal’s Existing Property Clause or Existing Property Extension.md
documents.excerpt: Extends cover to property already existing at the project site before contract works started.

headings.text: Crucial Application
What to check
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: principal’s existing property clause or existing property extension principal’s existing property clause or existing property extension.md policy & clauses crucial application what to check suggested wording endorsement clauses car contractor all risk meaning extends cover to property already existing at the project site before contract works started. this is different from contract works. without this extension, existing property damage may not be covered under car material damage section. crucial application apply when renovation / upgrading / extension works damage an existing building or existing structure. example: contractor renovating existing mall unit damages existing ceiling / wall / m&e system. construction works damage existing building owned by principal. fire starts from works and damages old building structure. what to check is principal’s existing property extension endorsed? what is the limit? is the damaged property existing property or contract works? was it at the insured project site? was damage caused by contract works? is there any exclusion for property being worked upon? suggested wording the damaged property appears to be existing property and not part of the contract works. we recommend insurers / operators confirm whether principal’s existing property extension is endorsed and the applicable limit. pending confirmation, policy liability is reserved.

documents.title: Property Damage Clarification Clause
documents.excerpt: Property damage means physical damage to the substance of property.

headings.text: Key Point
Application Sample
Facts
Suggested wording

documents.searchText: property damage clarification clause property damage clarification clause.md policy & clauses key point application sample facts suggested wording endorsement clauses simple meaning property damage means physical damage to the substance of property. damage to data / software alone may be excluded unless physical damage is involved. key point for it / electronic claims, separate physical equipment damage from data / software loss. application sample facts computer / server affected. insured claims for software corruption / data loss. no physical damage to hardware. suggested wording the policy is noted to contain property damage clarification clause. the clause requires physical damage to the substance of the insured property. in the absence of physical damage to the hardware, claim for data / software loss alone may fall outside the scope of property damage cover, subject to policy wording.
